docs.title: The Nomad Chronicles (TNC)
docs.text:
Welcome to the official rules site for The Nomad Chronicles TTRPG.

Use the navigation menu to explore:

  • Core Rules
  • Character Creation
  • Traits, Expertises, and Specializations
  • Equipment
  • Bestiary
  • GM Tools

Tip

This site is currently under development and updated regularly during playtesting.

docs.title: Items Properties Index
docs.text: This is a list of features common to various items and weapons, both to define items listed in this book, and in the event a player or GM wishes to create custom equipment. For particularly specific items, GMs are encouraged to adjust prices appropriately.

docs.title: Armor
docs.text: Active Camouflage: Specially woven to blend with the environment around it. Grants a Minor Advantage on Reflexes (Stealth) tests relying on remaining unseen. Covert: Can be worn under other clothing items. CR 10 + your Constitution score is required to spot the armor. Cut Resistant: Retains full AP against weapons that deal slashing or piercing damage. Extra Pockets: Gain a bonus +2 Units of Carrying Capacity. Hazard Seals: In the event of a puncture, armor with Hazard Seals automatically cinches off portions to keep a hermetic seal. Grants a Major Advantage on Saving Throws against airborne hazards.

docs.title: Weapons
docs.text: 5-Cylinder: Holds up to 5 bullets in the weapon at a time. Bullets are loaded into the cylinder at a rate of 1 Round per Minor Action. The cylinder revolves after every trigger pull, cycling the next loaded Round into the weapon's chamber. 6-Cylinder: Holds up to 6 bullets in the weapon at a time. Bullets are loaded into the cylinder at a rate of 1 Round per Minor Action. The cylinder revolves after every trigger pull, cycling the next loaded Round into the weapon's chamber. Acid Damage: When used against objects, it moves the item one step lower on the Item Destruction Chart per round until that Item is Completely Destroyed. When used against a biological target, it decreases Armor Protection (AP) from all sources by 1 per round. Worn armor at 0 AP is useless weight, and will no longer protect you from damage. For characters with the Hardened Skin Special Ability Mutation, lost AP is restored by 1 AP per Rest. Every Round after the initial hit, a creature can attempt a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 15 to end the effect. Any creature with an AP of 0 while affected by Acid takes 10 Damage at the start of every turn until they pass the Saving Throw. AP-Tuned: When fired at any non-living character or object, the loaded ammunition is treated as AP ammo, regardless of whether or not it's actually loaded, allowing the damage of the weapon to ignore an additional 6 AP provided by material. Armor-Piercing: Ignores up to 4 AP provided by armor or cover. Auto-Fire: The weapon is capable of firing in fully automatic bursts. When a weapon is used this way, it hits a 5-yard cone area, and uses 10 bullets. If the weapon has less than 10 bullets loaded, the size of the affected area decreases by 1 yard per 2 missing bullets. Ballistic Damage: Damage caused by firearms. Blast Damage: Damage caused by explosive weapons and devices. Unless otherwise specified, this damage hits a 4 yard radius area. Bleed: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Bleeding Condition. Bludgeoning Damage: Damage caused by blunt items and weapons. Box Magazine: A detachable magazine that can be inserted into a weapon as a Minor Action, providing Ammunition to be fired. Expended Ammunition Casings are usually ejected by the weapon in order to load the next bullet in the cycle. Burst-Fire: The weapon is capable of firing in short bursts with every pull of the trigger, spending 5 bullets, and granting a Minor Advantage to the Attack Roll. The weapon must have at least 5 bullets loaded to benefit from this bonus. A Character with the Rifleman Style Expertise can choose to expend only 3 bullets to gain the benefit, but the weapon must have 3 bullets loaded into it. Cheap Ammo: The weapon can use just about any object of the proper size as ammunition. This weapon's ammunition need not be tracked, as it's likely a character is surrounded by objects that can be used. Cold Damage: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Might Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Hypothermic Condition. Concealable: While this weapon/item is on your person and remains undrawn, a Perception check CR 10 + your Reflexes Score is required to spot the weapon. CP Boost: On rolling doubles, the weapon grants an additional bonus to Chance Points equal to half the value of the Chance Die. Double: The weapon has two damaging ends, allowing for a second attack as a Minor Action. Energy Damage: Damage caused by plasma-based weapons, which completely ignores AP provided by armor. Additionally, chance to cause Burning Condition. Must be GM-approved. Fast Reload: Reloading this weapon is a Free Action, leaving your Minor Action for other uses. Finesse: A weapon with this property can be used with either the Might or Reflexes Ability, user's choice. Fire Damage: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Reflexes Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Burning Condition. Flash: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Impaired Sense (Sight) Condition. Heavy: Imposes a -2 penalty to Reflexes when determining Movement Speed. High Caliber: On rolling triples, before determining Chance Actions, the attacker can spend 3 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10+ the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or the Damage dealt will not heal until the target has received Surgery. Improvised: An item with this property has primary functions that have nothing to do with weaponry or causing damage. Internal Magazine: The magazine is an integral part of the weapon, and cannot be removed. Rounds can be fed into it at a rate of 1 Round per Minor Action, up to its full Ammunition Capacity. Those Rounds are then cycled by the action of the weapon. Ionic Damage: A character hit by an Ionic weapon must make a Constitution Saving Throw CR 10 + the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain a number of Tiers of the Stunned Condition equal to the Target's Chance Die (a result of 6 allows the Target to ignore the Condition). Electronic Equipment hit by this type of damage becomes inoperable for 5 Minutes (20 Rounds). If said item is hit by Ionic Damage a second time before the time it needs to reboot, it becomes inoperable until repaired. Knockdown: This weapon delivers a significant amount of force to a target. On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or fall Prone. Leverage: Weapons and items with this property grant a Major Advantage (+5) when making a Might (Strength) Action Roll to pry open or break an object. Piercing Damage: Damage caused by bows, and some pointed weapons. Precise: Attack rolls made within a weapon's CQB, Short, or Medium Range Increments gain an additional +2 bonus to the Attack Roll. Reach: Allows a weapon to be used in Melee Range at up to 4-yards away. Reliable: The weapon never jams, and only misfires on a roll of 3 on 3d6, regardless of doubles. Safety Switch: The weapon can be made not to fire, allowing for safer handling of the firearm. Using the switch is a Minor Action. Select-Fire: The weapon has a switch that allows its firing mode to be changed. The firing modes available are detailed in the weapon's description. Switching between firing modes is a Minor Action, and your firing mode must be chosen before you make your attack roll. Semi-Auto: Every pull of the trigger fires a single bullet from the chamber of the weapon. Silent: The weapon makes no noise, and does not break Stealth when used. Single Shot: The weapon must be primed each time it's fired, requiring a Minor Action to feed a round into the chamber. Slashing Damage: Damage caused by weapons with a bladed edge. Sonic: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Impaired Sense (Hearing) Condition. Splash: Effect or Damage is spread across an area. Unless otherwise specified, this spread is 4 yards. Spray: If the weapon this property is applied to is used to make an Auto-Fire attack, the attack roll suffers a Minor Disadvantage (-2), but increases the damage cone size by 5 yards. Spread-Fire: The weapon fires in a cone of damage. The size of the cone is dependent on the Range the weapon is fired from, the width of which is equal to the weapon's range increment. Each range increment reduces the damage by 2. Stun: On rolling doubles, before determining Chance Actions, the attacking character can spend 1 CP. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw, CR 10 + the value on the Attacker's Chance Die, or gain 1 Tier of the Stunned Condition. Thrown: The weapon can be thrown with some precision. Weapons without this property that are thrown are treated as Improvised Ranged Weapons. Trigger Safety: The Safety Switch is a part of the trigger mechanism, that prevents accidental firing while allowing the weapon to be fired without hesitation. Two-Handed: The weapon cannot be properly used with fewer than two hands. Unreliable: The weapon is prone to malfunctions. The weapon misfires on a 5 or 6 on 3d6, and jams on a 3 or 4 on 3d6. Versatile: This weapon can be used with one or two hands. If used with two hands, add the Minimum Might Requirement as a bonus to the Weapon's Damage.

docs.title: Explosives
docs.text: Bleed: Applies one or more Tiers of the Bleeding Condition on a failed save. The number of Tiers applied is specified in the weapon's description. Blind: Applies T3 of the Impaired Sense (Sight) Condition on a failed save. This effect decreases by 1 Tier at the beginning of each of the target's subsequent turns. Color-Code: Weapon can be purchased in a variety of colors to fit various tactical communication scenarios. Concealment: Weapon creates an area of visual concealment. Attacking a completely concealed character adds +5 to that target's Defense. Destructive: When damaging non-organic targets, ignores up to 20 AP before determining the results of damage. Directional: Has a front that can be placed, so that the explosive can fire in a cone of damage ahead of it. Ion Blast: Applies the effects of Ionic Damage as if it was Blast Damage. Unless otherwise specified, this damage hits a 4 yard radius area, ignoring any cover not shielded against EMP attacks, but does not affect organic targets. Shapeable: Weapon can be molded into various shapes, allowing it to be directional if needed, focusing the weapon's damage into specific points. When used this way, the user removes a number of unintended targets from their Area of Effect equal to their Wits Ability. Spread: The contents of this weapon spread at a rate of 1-yard per turn, causing its effects within the cloud area. This cloud can go around corners and through gaps at least an inch wide, but can be dispersed by heavy winds. Timed: This weapon explodes following the next Action after being used. Multiple applications extend this time by 1 Action, up to 1 minute.

docs.title: Vehicles
docs.text: All-Terrain: Ignores difficult terrain penalties for most natural environments (mud, sand, snow, etc.), but reduces Top Speed by 1 category. Autonomous: The vehicle has an onboard AI capable of basic self-navigation, though it still requires a human operator for combat maneuers and other complex actions. Electric Vehicle (EV): Requires at least 1 hour of charging per hour of use. Maximum of 6 hours on a full charge. Fuel Efficient: Consumes fuel at half the rate of a standard model of its type, but reduces the Top Speed by 1 category. High Performance: Consumes fuel at double the rate of a standard model of its type, but increases Top Speed by 1 category. Manual Transmission: Requires additional time to shift gears, but allows better speed control. All maneuvers are Major Actions, but reduce Speed penalties to Maneuver by 3. Ruggedized: Designed for durability, this vehicle reduces incoming damage from collisions by 1 category at the expense of Top Speed (A collision at Highway Speed deals Street Speed damage). Tow Hook: Vehicle can carry up to an additional half of its standard Cargo capacity.

docs.title: Extra Tables
docs.text: Mission Title: Operation #: Objective Summary Estimated Time Hours Days Weeks Months Downtime Table Roll (1d10) Category DowntimeDescription 1-3 Short 2d6 hours or 1d6 days. An urgent follow-up mission with little time for recovery. 4-6 Moderate 1d6+1 Weeks Standard rest and preparation period. 7-9 Long 2d6 Weeks Enough time to regroup, investigate leads, or heal. 10 Extended 1d6+1 Months A longer break, possibly after a major event or injury. Shift Season every 3 months. docs.title: Chapter 5: Chance Actions
docs.text:
Chance Actions cover a wide array of potential maneuvers a character can attempt, whether in or out of combat. They are fueled from a pool of Chance Points, built up by rolling Doubles and Triples on the dice.

Doubles and Triples

  • Chance Points gained by rolling Doubles is equal to the value of the Chance Die.

  • Chance Points gained by rolling Triples is equal to the value of the Chance Die +6.

GM Tip - Chance Pool

At your discretion, players begin every Game Session with 6 Chance Points in their pool, ready to be used.

Unless specifically stated otherwise, the vast majority of Chance Actions are used in conjunction with a Major Action (such as an Attack, Grapple, or Action Roll).

Detailed below are descriptions, rules, and examples of the Chance Actions you can potentially perform during a game session. These Chance Actions are offered as a way to make Action Rolls more dynamic.

Chance Actions are not bound by type.

Note: While the Chance Actions are separated into multiple lists, they are not mutually exclusive. If you can justify a Non-Combat Chance Action in a combat situation, then you can spend the CP required to perform the action. Likewise, if you can somehow justify using a Combat Chance Action in a less than hostile situation, then you can spend the CP required to perform it.

docs.title: General Combat
docs.text: Assessment (1+ CP): Choose a single target. As a Free Action, make a Perception (Empathy) Action Roll CR 10 + your target's Wits (Deception). If you win, ignore up to 5 points of your Target's total Defense. Add one additional target per CP spent. Acrobatic Bluff (1+ CP): Gain up to a +3 bonus to your Defense by spending a Minor Action and rolling a Reflexes (Acrobatics) Action Roll CR 10 + your target's Perception (Empathy). Brace (1+ CP): When subjected to a Saving Throw, you can spend an Instinct Action to increase your roll against that Saving Throw by 1 per CP spent. Team Attack (2 CP): On a successful attack, choose an ally that can see and hear you within 6 yards. That ally can use an Instinct Action to immediately attack the same enemy, with a +1 bonus. Taunt (2 CP): Roll a Willpower (Deception) Action Roll as a Minor Action versus Perception (Empathy) or Willpower (Self-Discipline) of any target within 10 yards. If you win, any aggressive actions made by that opponent must target you for the next minute (4 rounds). Back-to-Back (2 CP): You bolster an adjacent ally's defense, providing both of you with a +1 bonus to Defense until the start of your next turn. Savage Striker (2 CP): Your attack does an extra +2 Damage. Precision Attack (2 CP): Your attack ignores up to 2 AP provided by equipment. Evasive Maneuvers (3 CP): As an Instinct Action, you can impose a -2 penalty on an opponent's Ranged Attack Roll.[evaluation required] Rapid Attack (3 CP): You can make one additional attack, either against an enemy you are already engaged with, or a different target within range and sight. You must have a loaded Ranged weapon to make a Ranged Attack. Doubles on this attack do not produce more CP. Inflict Wounds (3 CP): Your attack deals 1/2 its Base Damage but attempts to inflict a single Tier 1 Condition, based on damage type (i.e.: Bleeding, Winded, Weakened). The target must roll a Constitution Saving Throw CR 8 + your Chance Die or suffer the Condition. Heavy Hitter (6 CP): Your attack ignores up to 5 AP provided by Equipment. Shocking Brutality (6 CP): If you take out an opponent, you can spend your Instinct Action. Any opponents within 6 yards to witness the attack must roll a Willpower Saving Throw CR 10 + your Chance Die from the attack roll. On a successful Save, the targets gain Tier 1 of the Fear Condition. On a failed Save, Tier 3. Lethal Blow (6 CP): Your attack does an extra +5 Damage. Close Call (9 CP): If an attack would take you to 0 Health, you fall Unconscious with 1 Health instead. Last Stand (9 CP): After falling to 0 Health, you can activate this Chance Action as an Instinct Action. Doing so gives you a single round where you ignore all Damage and Conditions. During this round, no Healing Action Rolls work to your benefit, and you fall to Dying with 1d6 rounds removed from the Dying timer.

docs.title: Melee
docs.text: Footwork (1+ CP): On a successful hit, move yourself or your opponent up to 2 yards per CP spent in any direction. Upward movement is halved. Parry (1+ CP): As an Instinct Action, you make an opposed Melee Attack Roll against your opponent. If you win, your next Melee Attack against that opponent gains a bonus equal to the CP spent into this Action. Bullying Blow (2 CP): On a successful hit, you can attempt to inflict the Fear Condition on an opponent as a Free Action. Your opponent must make a Willpower Saving Throw CR 10 + your Chance Die, gaining the next tier of the Condition on a failure. Disarm (2 CP): You attempt to disarm the target with your melee attack. You and your opponent must make an opposed attack roll. These attack rolls do not generate Chance Points. If you win the test, you knock your enemy’s weapon a number of yards away equal to your Chance Die result + Might. Trip (2 CP): Instead of dealing damage, make an opposed Might (Strength) test. If you win, your Melee Attack knocks your enemy prone. Iron Fist (3 CP): Ignore up to 2 AP from natural sources. This does not ignore AP provided by Armor. Bind Up (3 CP): As an Instinct Action, you make an opposed Melee Attack Roll against your opponent. If you win, you may immediately make an Unarmed Attack against them. Riposte (3 CP): If an opponent misses you with a Melee Attack before the start of your next turn, you can make an immediate Melee Counterattack against them as an Instinct Action, adding your Wits to the Damage Total. Vicious Stomp (6 CP): Make an immediate attack against a target that falls prone within your reach as an Instinct Action. If you hit, add your Chance Die to the Damage (this stacks with the bonuses from rolling Doubles and Triples). Whirlwind Assault (6 CP): Make a series of attacks against all enemies within range of your Melee Attack. Make a single Attack Action Roll, comparing it to each enemy's Defense, resolving Damage as normal. Devastating Combination (6 CP): You make additional Melee Attacks in rapid succession. Make a single Attack Action Roll with a +2 bonus to the roll. You land a number of hits equal to the value on your Chance Die, adding that amount to your Damage Total.

docs.title: Ranged
docs.text: Covering Fire (1+ CP): When an opponent targets any creature other than you, make a Ranged Attack Action Roll against it as an Instinct Action. If you miss, that opponent suffers a Minor Disadvantage (-2) on their Attack Action Roll. If you hit, that opponent suffers a Major Disadvantage (-5) on their Attack Action Roll. Stock Striker (1 CP): Make a Melee Attack with your Firearm. Gain a +1 bonus to the Attack Action Roll when you use a two-handed firearm. On a success, your target must make a Might Saving Throw CR 10 + your Chance Die or fall Prone and Stunned. Strafe (2 CP): While using a firearm with the Autofire property, you target a 10 yard cone. Opponents in the area up to your weapon's Long Range must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 10 + your Perception Ability, taking your weapon's normal Damage on a failure. Overwatch (3 CP): Designate a target behind full cover that you have already perceived. If that target moves before the start of your next turn, Roll a Ranged Attack Action Roll as an Instinct Action. Tactical Takedown (6 CP): Whenever a target falls Prone within your CQB or Short Range, you can make a single Ranged Attack against that target as an Instinct Action. On a successful hit, add twice your Chance Die to the Damage (this stacks with the bonuses from rolling Doubles and Triples). One Shot Left (12 CP): As long as you have only one piece of ammunition loaded in your weapon and on your person, you can spend an Instinct Action. If you do, gain a +5 bonus to your Base Damage, and add 3x your Chance Die to the Damage (this stacks with the bonuses from rolling Doubles and Triples). Headshot (18 CP): You spend an Instinct Action, a Free Action, and a Minor Action, to line up a devastating shot. On a successful hit, deal Critical Damage x2.

docs.title: Grappling
docs.text: Shoulder Toss (1+ CP): Roll a Grapple Action Roll. If you succeed, you throw the target Prone in any space you threaten, or up to 1 yard away per CP spent. This ends the Grapple. Catch Attack (2 CP): Roll an opposed Attack Action Roll against Melee or Thrown Attacks as an Instinct Action. If you succeed against a Melee Attack, your opponent is Grappled. If you succeed against a Thrown Attack, you can choose to have the weapon in your hand, ready for use. Desperate Grab (3 CP): On any attack (yours or your opponent's) that ends with a Near-Miss, you can spend an Instinct Action to initiate a Grapple. Binding Throw (3 CP): On a successful Throw Action Roll, you initiate a Lock Action Roll as an Instinct Action. Your target makes a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 10 + your Chance Die. Deadly Grapple (6 CP): While you are in control of a Grapple, you can make an Attack Action Roll as a Minor Action with a +3 bonus to your Base Damage. This attack ends the Grapple. Bone Breaker (12 CP): While you are in control of a Grapple, you initiate a Lock Action Roll with a Minor Disadvantage (-2). Your target makes a Reflexes or Might Saving Throw (target's choice) CR 10 + your Chance Die. On a successful Save, the Target suffers the Weakness (T2) Condition until they are able to take a Rest. On a failed save, the target suffers Weakness (T4) until they are able to get Surgery. Chokehold (12 CP): While you are in control of a Grapple, you can initiate a Chokehold as a Major Action. Your target makes a Constitution Saving Throw CR 10 + your Might Ability. On a failed save, the target gains Fatigue (T1). The target makes the Saving Throw every round you maintain the Chokehold with a Major Action. On a successful save of 2 or more, they break free of the Grapple. Sickle and Hammer (12 CP): Make an opposed Attack Roll against an incoming Melee Attack. If you succeed, your target's attack fails and you initiate a Grapple, dealing Unarmed Critical Damage. Neckbreaker (18 CP): While you are in control of a Grapple, you initiate a Lock Action Roll as a Major Action. Your target makes a Constitution Saving Throw CR 10 + your Chance Die. On a successful Save, the target suffers the Incapacitated Condition until they are able to get Surgery. On a failed Save, the target suffers the Paralyzed Condition and begins Suffocating.

docs.title: Chapter 3: Character Creation
docs.text:
"Every Nomad starts with scars -- it's the ones you choose that define you."

The character you play in The Nomad Chronicles may appear to be a series of numbers and mathematical formulae on a sheet of power. What it is, however, is your eyes and ears into the world.

There are six steps to character creation, each one a straightforward extension from the step before it.

Creation Steps Quick Reference

  1. Choose a Concept
  2. Determine Ability Scores
  3. Select a Background
  4. Choose Traits, Expertise, & Specializations
  5. Pick or Purchase Equipment
  6. Finalize and Calculate Derived Stats (Health, Speed, Defense, etc)

docs.title: Step 1: Concept
docs.text:
Every character starts as an idea. Who they are, what drives them, and what kind of survivor they're on track to become.

Think in Arcs

Before numbers hit the page, think personality, motivation, and aesthetic.

  • What keeps this character moving?

  • What broke them before the story began?

  • How do they fit with the group?

docs.title: Step 2: Ability Scores
docs.text: Ability Scores are the backbone of how your character moves and operates through the world, and are used to determine your character's physical and mental prowess.

docs.title: Determining Abilities
docs.text:
When you're ready, take your 3d6 and roll them, adding them together and comparing that result to the Determining Abilities Table. On the left side of that table are the various sum results of rolling 3d6. On the right side is where you'll find the Ability Scores you'll record on your sheet.

You'll need to make six total rolls. One per Ability. You may roll in order, starting from the top.

After rolling as standard, your GM may allow you to swap up to two Ability Scores of your choice, to better customize your concept.

GM Tip

Optional Ruling: Like in certain dungeon-crawling systems, you could also simply allow your players to roll six times, record the results, and apply those results to whichever Ability Scores they fancy for specific build types.

Abilities Table

Roll Result Starting Ability 3 -2 4 -1 5 -1 6 0 7 0 8 0 9 1 10 1 11 1 12 2 13 2 14 2 15 3 16 3 17 4 18 4

docs.title: Exceptional Start
docs.text:
If, while rolling Ability Scores, a player rolls Doubles, they may add up to half of the value of their Chance Die to the result of their roll for determining their starting score. If they roll Triples, they may add up to the full value of their Chance Die to up to two different Ability Scores. No Ability Score can go above 6 at Character Creation in this way. GM-Tip (Scores above 5)

Any player that wants an Ability Score above 5 must first take at least 1 Infection Level to represent the Nano-Virus affecting that particular Score. 5 is peak human capacity.

docs.title: Standard Array
docs.text:
Instead of leaving their characters in the hands of fate with a roll, a player may wish to start play with a Standard Array of Ability Scores. There are two options in this particular setup, depending on how specialized or versatile a player would like their character to be.

Array 1 Array 2 +4 +3 +2 +3 +2 +2 +1 +1 0 0 -1 -1

Understanding High Scores

The pages below detail what having an Ability Score at certain values could mean for a character. A 0 is average, and is representative of any number of NPC capabilities. A 5 is peak capability for any human character, with any score above it counting as Super Human. No score can go above 5 without at least 1 point in Infection Level. A 10 is the game cap, and is a generally unheard of level of ability.

docs.title: Step 3: Background
docs.text:
The seven Backgrounds available represent a wide swath of personal or professional inclinations and training. They encompass many common personal experiences and walks of life. They are balanced based on what individuals in those professions sociotypically encounter and work with in their everyday lives. If your table requires more specific experiences or options, be sure to discuss the particulars with your GM.

Characters also do not generally start with much. Through playing, your character will have opportunities to improve their equipment but typically start with a few basics. Roll 2d6 and add your Background's RP Bonus for your starting RP amount. If you roll doubles, roll your Chance Die, and add it to the result. [See Chapter 7: The Armory, for more RP rules, and equipment.]

That said, with every Background is a list of possible starting benefits. A player may pick 1 of these benefits to go along with the other, automatic benefits of their Background. Optionally, the player may also roll 2d6 to randomly determine their starting benefit. The player may choose or roll another benefit at Levels 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20.

docs.title: Athlete
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Might, Reflexes, Constitution Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 3d6 Starting RP: +40 Weapon Groups: Brawling, Light Melee Add 1 to your Reflexes Ability Pick one of the following Traits: Athletic (Might, Reflexes, or Constitution), Acrobatics (Reflexes), Running (Constitution), or Climbing (Might)

docs.title: Athlete Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Constitution 3-4 Trait: Martial Arts (Might or Reflexes) 5 Trait: Swimming (Constitution) 6 Trait: Strength (Might) 7-8 +1 Reflexes 9 Weapon Group: Archaic Ranged 10-11 +1 Might 12 Weapon Group: Archaic Melee

docs.title: Blue Collar
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Might, Constitution, Wits Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 2d6 Starting RP: +40 Weapon Groups: Light Melee, Heavy Melee Add 1 to your Constitution Ability Pick one of the following Traits: Strength (Might), Jury-Rigging (Wits), or Driving (Reflexes)

docs.title: Blue Collar Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Might 3-4 Trait: Smithing (Might) 5 Trait: Electrical (Wits) 6 Trait: Engineering (Wits) 7-8 +1 Wits 9 Weapon Group: Crafting (Reflexes) 10-11 Lock-Picking (Reflexes) 12 +1 Reflexes

docs.title: Law Enforcement
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Might, Reflexes, Perception Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 2d6 Starting RP: +50 Weapon Groups: Brawling, Light Melee, Small-Arms Add 1 to your Perception Ability Pick one of the following Traits: Running (Constitution), Persuasion (Willpower), or Leadership (Willpower)

docs.title: Law Enforcement Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Constitution 3-4 Trait: Martial Arts (Might or Reflexes) 5 Trait: Willpower (Bargaining) 6 Trait: Research (Wits) 7-8 +1 Might 9 Traits: Military Lore (Wits) 10-11 +1 Willpower 12 +1 Expertise

docs.title: Medicine
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Wits, Willpower, Perception Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 2d6 Starting RP: +80 Weapon Groups: none Add 1 to your Willpower Ability Traits: Empathy (Perception), Natural Lore (Wits), Healing (Wits)

docs.title: Medicine Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Wits 3-4 Trait: Searching (Perception) 5 Trait: Historical Lore (Wits) 6 Trait: Surgery (Willpower) 7-8 +1 Constitution 9 Trait: Courage (Willpower) 10-11 Trait: Self-Discipline (Willpower) 12 +1 Willpower

docs.title: Military
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Might, Reflexes, Willpower Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 3d6 Starting RP: +60 Weapon Groups: pick to between: Light Melee, Heavy Melee, Small-Arms, Long-Arms, Heavy-Arms, Stealth Weapons Add 1 to your Might Ability Pick one of the following Traits: Courage (Willpower), Knives (Reflexes), Martial-Arts (Might), Grappling (Might or Reflexes), Basic Training (Any)

docs.title: Military Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Constitution 3-4 Trait: Stealth (Reflexes) 5 Trait: Explosives Lore (Wits) 6 Trait: Courage (Willpower) 7-8 +1 Willpower 9 Self-Discipline (Willpower) 10-11 +1 Perception 12 +1 Expertise

docs.text: Primary Abilities: Wits, Willpower, Perception Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 1d6 Starting RP: +30 Weapon Groups: none Add 2 to your Wits Ability Traits: Computer Use (Wits), Cryptography (Wits), Research (Wits)

docs.title: Scholar Starting Benefits
docs.text: 2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Willpower 3-4 Trait: Faith (Willpower) 5 Trait: Crafting (Reflexes) 6 Trait: Electrical (Wits) 7-8 +1 Perception 9 Trait: Stamina (Constitution) 10-11 Trait: Cultural Lore (Wits) 12 +1 Wits

docs.title: White Collar
docs.text: Primary Abilities: Wits, Perception, Willpower Starting Health: 10 + Constitution + 1d6 Starting RP: +120 Weapon Groups: none Add 1 to your Willpower Ability Traits: Bargaining (Wits or Willpower), Etiquette (Willpower), Evaluation (Wits)

docs.title: White Collar Starting Benefits
docs.text:
2d6 Benefit 2 +1 Willpower 3-4 Trait: Morale (Willpower) 5 Trait: Persuasion (Willpower) 6 Trait: Writing (Wits) 7-8 +1 Wits 9 Traits: Deception (Willpower or Wits) 10-11 Traits: Empathy (Perception) 12 +1 Reflexes Optional Starting Traits

Need more flexibility in your background? Ask your GM about these optional Background Bonuses. Note that picking one of these replaces the Starting Benefit (at minimum) of the Background you've chosen.

  • Improv Expert: Once per Rest, pick a number of Traits you do not have equal to your Wits Ability, gaining a +2 bonus, as though they would in a Secondary Ability.

  • Student of All: Instead of gaining the default benefits of your Background, pick a single element from each of the available Backgrounds to start with.

  • Basic Training: Any time you would gain the Basic Tier of an Expertise, you may gain an additional Basic Tier of another Expertise.

docs.title: Chapter 6: Hazards, Conditions, & Virus Mode
docs.text:
There are many ways a character can come to harm, that aren't necessarily weapon related. Following are common forms of difficulties that can arise even without combat.

Hazards experienced in this way can be rated by their potency, which could in turn, correspond to a relative Threat Level. The CRs against these hazards are set by the GM based on the severity of hazard, but in general, any effect that calls for a saving throw has a CR of 10 + Threat Level.

docs.title: Poisons
docs.text:
Poisons can be broken down into five categories, based on their delivery method. This could be a dose of Curare slathered onto the blade of a knife, the bite of a venomous King Cobra, or a canister of Tear Gas.

All Poisons have two effects. On application of a poison, the target immediately suffers the first effect, and gains 1 Poisoned Tier. If left uncured for 5 minutes (20 rounds), the character suffers the second effect and gains another Tier. Following this, every 5 minutes the Poison has not been treated, the Poisoned Condition automatically increases another Tier.

Several sample poisons, and their effects, are listed in Chapter 7: The Armory.

Contact A contact poison is one that delivers its effects through touch alone. On contact, the character gains one Poison Tier and makes a Constitution Saving Throw. A contact poison will have the same effects if it is ingested. Infections Infections are linked, primarily, to bodily injury. If a character does not have proper medical treatment after being injured, even if they rest, they must make a Constitution Saving Throw. Success on this roll means the character heals as normal. On a failure, the character immediately gains Poisoned (T1) Condition. The character makes this save at the end of every Rest, ending its progression on a success. Ingested Poisons that must be ingested to be dangerous are often applied to food or drink that their target may consume. Inhaled These poisons are those that are often gases, fumes, and other noxious substances. If the character can see the affected area (such as with a Tear Gas Grenade) then they can opt to hold their breath to move through it. Keep in mind, however, that a character can only hold their breath for so long. - refer to suffocation Injury Injury poisons are those that must be administered into the bloodstream to be effective. These are things like venomous snake bites and poisonous needle traps.

docs.title: Treating Poison
docs.text: A character trained in Healing, Poison Lore, or Natural Lore can identify the symptoms of poisoning on sight. Following that, they must make a Wits Action Roll CR 15 to identify the poison. As long as they have crafting material, the character may then make a Healing Action Roll CR 11 to craft and administer the correct anti-toxin. When a character receives the anti-toxin, all of their Poisoned Tiers are removed, one Tier per Rest.

docs.title: Environmental Hazards
docs.text: Acids and Bases These are caustic chemicals that can cause burns and scarring. They tend to be somewhat viscous, sticking to the body unless thoroughly washed away. Acid and Basic chemicals come in levels of potency, with each potency dealing different amounts of damage per round, at the beginning of the affected character's turn, depending on whether it's splashed on or the subject of it is immersed. As a general rule, the immersion damage is ten times the amount of splash damage at the same potency.

docs.title: Acids & Bases
docs.text: Threat Tier Potency Damage Low Mild 1/10 Moderate Corrosive 2/20 High Potent 3/30 Nightmare Concentrated 4/40 Extreme Cold With preparation, a character can generally go some time in sub-zero environments. But there's only so many layers a person can put on. And there's only so long those layers will last in a blizzard. After an hour of exposure to Extreme Cold, whether by their equipment failing or simply being unprepared, the character must make a Constitution Saving Throw CR 8. This CR increases by 2 for every additional hour of exposure. On a failed save, the character gains the Slowed Condition. The following hour, if still in the elements, the character makes another Constitution Saving Throw with the same progression as above, gaining the Hypothermic Condition on a failure. A character who has not found shelter for an additional hour falls Prone and Helpless. Extreme Heat A character may find themselves in a desert op, or a factory or forge at some point. Either way, in similar fashion to an Extreme Cold environment, a character entering an Extreme Heat environment, should they have protection, can last longer than one who's unprepared. After an hour of exposure to Extreme Heat, whether by failing equipment or a lack of preparation, the character must make a Constitution Saving Throw CR 8. This CR increases by 2 for every additional hour of exposure. On a failed save, the character gains Winded Condition. The following hour, if still in the Extreme Heat environment, the character makes another Constitution Saving Throw with the same progression as above, gaining the Overheated Condition on a failure. A character who has not found a way to cool off after an additional hour falls Prone and Helpless. Falling/Impact Should the character be struck by falling debris, or they are the debris, they may take damage depending on the situation. On an Impact due to structural collapse, or other such events, the struck character takes damage equal to the AP of the falling item, adding +1 Damage for each size category larger it is than the character. Subtracting -1 Damage for each size category smaller than the character. As for falling, a character who falls from a significant height may suffer damage depending on how far they fall. The character takes 1 Bludgeoning Damage for every 3 yards fallen, to a maximum of 10, in addition to damage equal to the AP Value of the material they fall onto. A character can attempt a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 10 +1 for every 3 yards fallen, success removing the material amount from the damage taken. Fire Similar to how Acids & Bases work, fires deal damage based on their size and severity. Whether this is due to direct contact or fire-related injuries such as smoke inhalation, a fire can be dangerous, even for a regenerating character. A Scalding or higher tier fire directly contacted will cause the character to gain the Burning Condition, along with suffering the damage of the fire itself.

docs.title: Fire
docs.text: Threat Tier Severity Damage Low Singing 1/10 Moderate Scalding 2/20 High Searing 3/30 Nightmare Destructive 4/40 Suffocation/Drowning Sometimes, the danger is simply not having enough oxygen. A character can hold their breath for a number of rounds equal to their Constitution Ability. Every round following this time requires a Constitution Saving Throw CR 8 +1 for every additional round. Failure on this save causes the character to take their Constitution Ability in Damage that ignores AP from all sources. If a character has time to prepare, the number of rounds they can hold their breath before having to make saving throws is extended by an amount equal to their Might Ability.

docs.title: Conditions
docs.text: Throughout a game, it is possible to suffer from a number of debilitating Conditions, which are covered in this section. Conditions can be the result of environmental factors, but can also be caused by damage. All effects from Conditions occur at the start of the affected character's turn. Following is a list of potential effects a character might encounter in any given game, sorted by severity and duration. Unless otherwise stated, values among Tiers don't stack. The character simply suffers the worst Tier they are affected by.

docs.title: Condition Severity Levels
docs.text:
Tier: Effective Threat Level Severity Duration 1: Trivial Minor Inconvenience Ends at the start of the attacking character's next turn or after 1 minute (4 rounds). 2: Low Mild Debilitation Ends after 1 minute (4 rounds) or a Breather. 3: Moderate Noticeable Impairment Requires Medical Treatment and/or a Rest. 4: High Severe Hindrance Requires Medical Intervention and/or extended downtime. 5: Nightmare Catastrophic Permanent or requires extreme Medical Intervention

Sudden Death

If a character gains 10 or more Condition Markers, in any combination, their body could potentially go into shock, killing them. At the 10th Marker, players roll a Constitution Saving Throw CR 10, adding +1 to the Save for every additional Marker over the 10th.

Revision needed beyond this point.

docs.title: Infection Levels & Virus Mode
docs.text:
As a Nomad, you have a strain of the Nano-Virus flowing through your bloodstream. It might give you an edge. Or it might be your downfall.

This section outlines the risks and rewards of embracing your infection through Virus Mode—a temporary state that enhances your capabilities at the cost of control.

docs.title: Infection Level (IL)
docs.text:
At character creation, a Nomad starts at Infection Level 0. From there, you may increase your Infection Level by spending Survivor Points (or via narrative developments).

Each new level grants:

  • A floating pool of Ability Score points (usable only during Virus Mode).
  • 1 Mutation per level, rolled randomly, or chosen secretly by the GM.

Each new level also requires the player to make an immediate Control Check.

Ability Score Points can only be distributed once per activation.

docs.title: Virus Mode Activation
docs.text:
Virus Mode can be activated:

  • Voluntarily, as a Free Action (once per Rest)
  • Involuntarily, if:
    • You enter the Dying state and have Regeneration or receive Healing.
    • You are suffering both Confusion (T3) and Enraged (T3)
    • The GM deems it appropriate (trauma, overwhelming stress, etc.)
Virus Mode Overview IL Transformation Ability Bonus Mutations Duration 1–5 Talon +1 1 per Level 1 minute (4 rounds) 6–8 Akuma +2 1 per Level 1 minute (4 rounds) 9–10 Leviathan +3 1 per Level 2 minutes (8 rounds)

docs.title: Control Check
docs.text:
Each time you activate Virus Mode, make a Willpower Save:

CR = 8 + Infection Level

A success means you're in full control. A failure begins a chain of adverse effects. Only one success is needed to halt escalation.

docs.title: Adverse Effect Escalation
docs.text:
Failure Count Effect 1st Failure Gain Confused (T4) 2nd Failure Also gain Enraged (T4) 3rd Failure Trigger Forced Transformation (unless IL 9 or higher)

If a character below IL 9 transforms, they become permanently unplayable—a bioweapon NPC.

Leviathan characters may bypass the Control Check, and can end Virus Mode early with a Major Action, once each per Rest.

docs.title: Post-Transformation Drawback
docs.text:
Virus Mode does not end cleanly.

Tier End Effect Talon/Akuma After 1 minute (4 rounds), Virus Mode ends. Roll the Chance Die. The total becomes the number of Condition Tiers applied to your character. These last for 10 minutes unless worsened. Leviathan After 2 minutes (8 rounds), or upon voluntarily ending it, roll the Chance Die and halve the result. Apply that number of Condition Tiers. These last for 5 minutes unless worsened.

Warning

Transformation at Infection Levels below 9 results in permanent character loss. Use Virus Mode carefully. High power carries high consequence.

GM Tip - Virus Mode

GM Tip: Virus Mode is a power spike. It’s meant to feel overwhelming—like lighting yourself on fire to burn down the battlefield. But it could also be your best chance when everything is falling apart.

Encourage it. Punish carelessness. Reward bravery. And never forget: control is earned, not given.

docs.title: Transformation Templates
docs.text: If a character loses control and transforms, the GM will use the following templates to determine their mutated state. These reflect raw, unstable mutations tailored to the character's Infection Level.

docs.title: Abilities (Traits)
docs.text: Number Trait +1 Might +1 Reflexes +1 Constitution –2 Wits –2 Willpower –2 Perception

docs.title: Attacks
docs.text: Weapon Attack Roll Damage Bone Claw +1 1

docs.title: Abilities (Traits)
docs.text: Number Trait +2 Might (Martial Arts) +2 Reflexes +2 Constitution –1 Wits –1 Willpower –1 Perception

docs.title: Attacks
docs.text: Weapon Attack Roll Damage Bone Claw +2 3

docs.title: Abilities (Traits)
docs.text: Number Trait +3 Might (Martial Arts) +3 Reflexes +3 Constitution +3 Wits +3 Willpower +3 Perception

docs.title: Attacks
docs.text: Weapon Attack Roll Damage Special Unarmed Attack +3 5 Armor-Piercing

docs.title: Special Ability Mutations
docs.text:
Acute Reflexes Gain +2 to Defense per instance of this Mutation (max +10). Adrenal Surge Gain +1 additional Minor and Major Action per round per instance (max +5 each). Aggressive Adaptation For each target you successfully hit during Virus Mode, reduce one active Condition by 1 Tier (max once per round) Analytical Senses Detect nearby infected creatures. Range = 10 yards × number of instances (max 100 yards). You may ask 1 question per instance (max 5). These must be answered truthfully and pertain to:
  • Approximate location
  • Infection Level
  • General health
  • Ability Scores

NOTE

This is not telepathy. Emotional state can be inferred, but not surface thoughts.

Cognitive Shielding Each instance allows you to ignore the Confused or Enraged Condition for 1 Round.

NOTE

This ability can be used in and out of Virus Mode.

Combat Instincts Gain +2 to Initiative per instance (max +10). Enhanced Durability Gain ×0.5 multiplier to Health per instance (cumulative). On the fifth instance, the multiplier becomes ×4 total.

NOTE

This only applies during Virus Mode.

Enhanced Senses Gain +2 to Perception Action Rolls per instance (max +10). Enhanced Speed Gain +2 yards of movement speed per instance (max +10). Enhanced Strength Gain +2 to all Melee Damage rolls per instance (max +10). Hardened Skin Gain +2 Armor Protection per instance (max +10), regardless of worn armor. Regeneration Regain (5 + Constitution) Health per minute. Reduce interval by 1 round (15 seconds) per instance (min 1 round).

Important

Outside of Virus Mode: Regain HP every 12 hours, reduced by 2 hours per instance (min 2 hours).

docs.title: Post-Leviathan Mutations
docs.text:
These mutations can only be gained after a character has reached Infection Level 9 or higher.

Flare Once per rest, you may instantly trigger full Virus Mode for 1 round as a Free Action. This activation does not require a transformation check and does not count against your normal Virus Mode usage. After this flare, Virus Mode ends automatically, and you suffer 2 random Tier 2 Conditions. Instinct Override Your physical body adapts to any threat without conscious input. Outside of Virus Mode, gain a +1 to all Saving Throws. While Virus Mode is active, this bonus increases to a +2. Additionally, once per Rest, you can declare a failed Save as a success instead. (You must declare this after rolling but before consequences are applied.) Low Pulse Your Virus Mode effects are passively active at half strength. You can attempt an opposed roll: Infection Level + 3d6 vs. enemy Perception (Analytical Senses) to remain hidden. Neural Overrun You can spend 1 Major Action to hijack the nervous system of a creature within 10 yards that you can accurately perceive. The target makes a Willpower Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Infection Level). On a failure, they suffer the Fear Condition (T3) for 1 Round, and Bleeding Condition (T2) for 1 minute (4 Rounds). Perfect Symbiosis You are immune to the Confused and Enraged Conditions while in Virus Mode. If your Virus Mode would force a Transformation, you can choose whether or not to allow it. Superior Adaptation Whenever you suffer a Condition, roll your Chance Die. On a 4 or above, you become immune to that Condition until the end of your next Rest. Virus Manipulation Once per rest, you may influence another character's use of Virus Mode, granting either a Major Advantage (+5) or Major Disadvantage (-5) to one of their Adverse Effect Rolls. Worldbreaker's Howl Once per Virus Mode activation, you can release a primal scream. All creatures within 10 yards that can see and hear you must make a Willpower Saving Throw (CR 12 + your Infection Level) or suffer the Fear Condition (T2) for 1 minute. Failure by 5 or more adds the Stunned Condition (T3). Failure by 10 or more additionally adds the Fatigue Condition (T4).

docs.title: Implements of Fate
docs.text: These are the tools you will need for play.

docs.title: Players:
docs.text:
Players only need three things to be ready for play. With the right foreknowledge on the character concept you'd like to play, a character sheet can be completely filled out in as little as five to ten minutes. Which, with the brutality inherent in this system, could potentially be important.

  1. Three six-sided die (3d6). Two of a single color , and a third of a contrasting color (or design). We'll discuss why a little later in this chapter.
  2. A pencil (or pen, if you like to live dangerously.) to adjust elements of your character.
  3. A character sheet, to hold all of your character's items, weapons, and abilities for reference.

docs.title: Game Masters:
docs.text:
A Game Master (GM) won't need much more than their players. The GM, however, does have a lot more optional tools at their disposal.

Needed:

  1. A physical or digital copy of this book.
  2. At least 3d6. Again, two of a single color, and a third of a contrasting color (or design).
  3. A single ten-sided die (d10).
  4. An Adventure (a few sample adventures have been provided in Chapter 9 of this book. Feel free to mix, match, and mash-up to your heart's content).

Optional

  1. A GM Screen. Some prefer the secrecy and natural tension provided by the GM screen. Others let the dice fall where they may, for all to see, and let their player's demises be recognized before they announce them.
  2. A second ten-sided die (d10), should you desire to roll percentile chance situations, create/use d100 lists.
  3. Maps and/or minis.

docs.title: Abilities and How to Use Them
A Brief Explanation of Abilities
docs.text:
There are six Ability scores, split between physical and mental prowess, listed below. The Average adult human's score is 0.

Throughout play you'll improve your skills, gaining Traits, Expertise, Specializations, and other abilities. Many of these modify the rolls you make with your Ability Scores, as bonuses and penalties.

Might This ability represents your character's physical strength. It covers such things as lifting heavy objects, carrying a friend, or grappling a foe. Reflexes This ability represents your character's general dexterity and is used to determine how hard they are to hit, whether through speed or effective movement through cover. Constitution This ability represents your character's health, endurance, and resistance to the physical dangers of the world. Wits This ability represents your character's acquired wealth of knowledge. It is useful in recalling information, planning, and understanding complex situations. Willpower This ability represents your character's applied experiences. It is a key component in applying one's skills to varying situations. Perception This ability represents your character's senses and hand-eye coordination.

docs.title: Action Rolls
docs.text:
3d6 + Ability + Trait

Whenever you want to do something, aside from basic movement and conversation, your GM is likely to ask you to make an Action Roll. Everything else in the game derives from the Action Roll. From firing a weapon, to driving a vehicle, to finding that key you dropped under the refrigerator.

During play, your GM will set the scene and then open the floor to the players who decide how to interact with what’s been described.

Dice are only necessary when there is a significant consequence for performing (or failing) an action.

To make an Action Roll, roll your 3d6. Two dice should be one color and the third a different color. The contrasting die is called the Chance Die. Add your relevant Ability Score, and an additional 2 if you have a related Trait, although the Trait is not necessary to attempt the roll. The final number is your test result, which you want to meet or exceed your Challenge Rating.

Even if you have several Traits that could apply, you can only use one on a test. You can never gain the benefits of more than one Trait on an Action Roll. These results are compared, either to a Challenge Rating set by the GM, or set by an opponent rolling against you.

docs.title: Making an Action Roll
docs.text:
When it is time to make a test, you are either rolling against a fixed Challenge Rating (CR) determined by the GM based on assumed difficulty, or an opponent's roll.

  1. You tell the GM what you are trying to do.
  2. The GM determines the ability and applicable Trait (if any). They then assign a CR to the task based on its difficulty and the prevailing circumstances.
  3. You make an Action Roll and figure out your result.
  4. If your test result is equal to or greater than the CR, you have successfully completed your action as intended.

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
The following table provides a common perception for test difficulty.

Also listed below are Advanced Action Rolls. These are provided for determining the effective Threat Level of non-combat situations, or for GMs who like to determine whether partial success or failure can apply to a roll.

Standard Task Difficulty

Difficulty CR Trivial: 7 Simple: 9 Average: 11 Challenging: 13 Hard: 15 Severe: 17 Imposing: 19 Near Impossible: 21

Advanced Task Difficulty (Success Threshold)

Difficulty Threshold Trivial: ≤ 5 Simple: 6-8 Moderate: 9-12 Difficult: 13-16 Professional: ≥ 17

docs.title: Advantage and Disadvantage
docs.text:
At times, your GM may decide a roll has been particularly affected by your circumstances. At that time, they will inform the player that their character has a minor or Major Advantage or Disadvantage. Following are the bonus(es)/penalty(ies) and examples.

2 or more sources of Minor Advantage/Disadvantage becomes a Major Advantage/Disadvantage. On the other hand, regardless of number, Major Advantages/Disadvantages do not stack in this way, although multiple instances and sources count when determining whether or not an Advantage or Disadvantage cancels its opposite out.

Minor Advantage/Disadvantage: +2 (2-on-1, target flanked, climbing using a rope)

Major Advantage/Disadvantage: +5 (3 or more on 1, target unaware, climbing using a ladder)

Things to remember about Action Rolls

  • Only one Trait can apply to a Action Roll.
  • Leave the dice on the table after a roll because the result of the Chance Die may be important.
  • In Opposed Action Rolls, ties are broken by the higher of the total of the 2 regular dice, by the Chance Die after, or by the higher ability if the Chance Dice are tied. If all else fails, the tie will go to the defender of the action.
  • For Basic Action Rolls, the GM needs to assess the situation and assign a difficulty.
  • Advanced Action Rolls use a success threshold to measure completion over time, or as a rough estimate of the effective Threat Level of non-combat situations.

docs.title: Chance Pool
docs.text:
As mentioned above, your off-color die is called the Chance Die. The number of Chance Points you receive is usually equal to the number that comes up on your Chance Die.

When doubles are rolled on certain types of tests, this generates Chance Points. These points go into a pool, to be used to fuel various special maneuvers called Chance Actions.

A Player Character's Chance Pool starts at 6 Chance Points at the beginning of every session, and caps at 18. Unused points are lost at the end of the session.

docs.title: Combat
docs.text:
When diplomacy breaks, and brutal force is the only outcome, the solution may be at the end of a barrel. For the inexperienced, this is a path to glory. For the seasoned, survival is victory enough. Yet, nothing can prepare you for the chaos of the battlefield: the deafening roar of gunfire, the thunderous shock of grenades, and the desperate cries of the wounded. In this unforgiving arena, you'll have to fight to win. To survive.

Combat is a cornerstone of The Nomad Chronicles, defined by intense and decisive moments. At its heart of course lies the Action Roll, but when lives hang in the balance, more is required. You'll need to understand how attacks are made, how damage is dealt, and what it takes to push a character to and beyond their limits.

docs.title: Initiative
docs.text:
Eventually, tensions will spike, and it will be important to make sense of what could be a chaotic battlefield. That's when the GM will tell the players to "Roll for Initiative."

Initiative Roll

Initiative = Reflexes + 3d6 + Modifiers

Once Initiative has started, time is tracked in 15-second increments called rounds. When the GM calls on you for your turn in the Initiative, you describe the various actions you'd like to attempt and roll for them. Once everyone has taken a turn, the round ends and a new one begins, until the end of the scenario that made Initiative necessary.

Time Conversions

1 round = 15 seconds

4 rounds = 1 minute

docs.title: Taking Actions
docs.text:
When it’s your turn in a round, you tell the GM what you want to do. Below are listed various actions that can be taken. The list is broken down into the least involved Non-Actions to the all-important Major Actions.

Unless otherwise stated, you can only perform one of each type of action in a round. You are not limited to the listed actions, but must discuss with your GM if what you'd like to do doesn't fall into one of these categories.

docs.title: Non-Actions
docs.text:
Non-Actions are activities that are done passively, don't require notable time or focus to perform, and in general consume no resources on your turn. These are often reflexive or incidental actions that flow within other actions, such as moving while speaking, or scanning the environment while standing guard.

MOVE: You can move up to your Speed in yards. You can also go prone, stand up, enter or exit a vehicle, but if you do so you can only move at half Speed (rounded down). If you start a round prone and do not stand up, you may only crawl, moving at half Speed. PERCEPTION CHECK: You can take a quick glance about, or listen passively in your environment without focusing on a specific detail. SHOUT!: You can make a brief, simple communication (such as a shout, warning, or command) of five words or less. CHANCE ACTION: You can use a Chance Action as an extension of a Major Action, unless otherwise described.

docs.title: Instinct Actions
docs.text:
Instinct Actions are swift, immediate responses to another character's actions, allowing you to act out of turn under specific conditions.

ATTACK OF OPPORTUNITY: If a target moves out of or through your melee reach, you can make a single melee attack roll against them. INTERCEPT: If an ally within 2 yards of you is attacked, you may attempt to move between the attacker and ally, redirecting the attack to yourself. QUICK DRAW: If equipped with a Quick-Draw Holster or similar, you can draw a weapon as an Instinct Action when you spot an opponent, letting you react to surprise encounters without delay.

docs.title: Free Actions
docs.text:
Free Actions are actions that you can perform on your turn without requiring significant time or focus.

QUICK DROP: Instantly release a held item, freeing up a hand. SHIFT GRIP: Change your grip on a weapon (e.g., switching from one-handed to two-handed or vice versa). SIMPLE GESTURE: Make a basic signal to an ally, such as pointing to a location or signaling to take cover. This could also be a spoken statement of about 25 words or less.

docs.title: Minor Actions
docs.text:
Minor Actions are small but significant movements aimed at maneuvering skillfully around the battlefield.

ACTIVATE: You must use this action to start using certain Traits, such as those with the Style tag. This action also allows you to use items. AIM: You take a moment to align your next strike. If your next action is an attack, you gain a Minor Advantage (+2) bonus on your next attack roll. JUMP: You can jump from a standing or moving position. A standing high or long jump has a total distance of your Might in yards. A jump while you're moving has a total distance of your Might + Reflexes in yards. PREPARE: You pick one major action that you're prepared to use, and what will trigger it, and then end your turn. Any time until your next turn, you can interrupt another character and take your prepared action immediately as an Instinct Action. If you don’t use it by your next turn, the action is lost. You can only take the Prepare action if you haven't already taken a major action. READY: You draw or holster a weapon or ready an item that was otherwise stowed for quick access. HOLD POSITION: You decide you won't be moved, planting yourself firmly. As long as you have not Moved, you can maintain Hold Position with a Minor Action for a number of Rounds equal to your Might (Strength). Any enemy who uses the Push-back or Knock Prone Chance Actions must succeed in an opposed test pitting their Might (Strength) vs. your choice of your own Might (Strength) or Reflexes (Acrobatics) in order to succeed.

When your opponent attempts to move you, should you win, the Chance Points they spent are still expended to no effect, regardless of how many Chance Points they spent in an attempt to move you (such as to move you more than 2 yards in a single attack with the Push-back Chance Action).

RUSH IN: You may move up to half your Speed (round down) in yards and then use a single Major Action. If this action is a melee attack against an adjacent enemy, you gain a +2 bonus on your melee damage total. TAKE COVER: Taking cover can be a Non-Action as part of your Initiative. Afterwards, it becomes a Minor Action if suitable cover (or a nearby object or individual) becomes readily available. The type and extent of cover provide varying levels of protection, but remember, cover might not always be practical or accessible. Choose your cover wisely, as a bullet might still have your name on it. 1/2 Cover: Gain a +2 bonus to Defense and Reflexes Saving Throws. 3/4 Cover: Gain a +5 bonus to Defense and Reflexes Saving Throws.

Quote

"They don't need to spot you to kill you, if they've got you cornered. Use cover to survive, but don't expect it to hide you forever." -- Captain Gil Thomas, Nomad Tactical Team 05-Bravo

Total Cover: You cannot be targeted by ranged attacks or abilities unless the attacker changes position or has a method to detect and bypass the cover. While in Total Cover, you may make a Blind Fire attack without exposing yourself. This attack suffers Major Disadvantage (-5), and cannot use Chance Actions or Chance Breaks. However, you may still benefit from the Chance Die, representing a lucky or instinctual hit. Attacking from Total Cover allows enemies to guess your position. If you did not Move when taking cover, an attacker may roll a Perception (Empathy) check vs your Reflexes (Stealth). On a success, they may attempt a single attack against you as though you had 3/4 Cover.

For detailed rules on using items and individuals as cover, refer to Chapter 6: The Armory.

Tip

In order to use an individual as improvised cover, the aggressor must first be in control of a grapple. The target character must be Entangled or Helpless in order to be used as cover. If an attack is made, and would miss the controlling character by 5 or less points, the attack hits the hostage used as cover, dealing normal damage to them.

Cover Table

Cover Type Defense & Save Bonus Other Effect 1/2 Cover +2 - 3/4 Cover +5 - Total Cover Cannot be targeted directly Blind Fire only (–5), no Chance Actions/Breaks. Can be targeted as 3/4 Cover if attacker succeeds on Perception (Empathy) vs Reflexes (Stealth) and the target did not Move.

docs.title: Major Actions
docs.text:
Major Actions are substantial, requiring the majority of your focus and time within a turn, like attacking or interacting with complex objects.

AID: You take a moment to assist an ally, granting them a Minor Advantage (+2) on a single Action Roll (combat or ability). BLIND FIRE: You throw caution to the wind, firing at your target without fully assessing their position. You suffer a Major Disadvantage (-5) to the attack roll, and cannot use Chance Actions or Chance Breaks with the attack. DEFEND: You brace yourself for incoming attacks, focusing entirely on self-preservation. Until the start of your next turn, you gain a +2 bonus to your Defense. GRAPPLE: You make a Melee Attack roll against an adjacent enemy. If you succeed, you and your target then engage in an opposed Might Action Roll. Success in this test grants you control of the grapple, applying the Entangled Condition to your target. While you control the grapple, you may:

  1. Choke: Invoke an additional opposed Might Action Roll. On a success, target gains 1 Tier of the Winded Condition. A choke can be held using a Minor Action at the start of each new turn the target hasn't escaped. Each turn the choke is held increases the Winded Condition by 1 Tier.

  2. Strike: Attack your target with an Unarmed attack or a light, one-handed weapon you possess or can draw. Targeting the Entangled Character: The attack is made with a Minor Advantage due to the close proximity. The penalty for Called Shots is reduced by one step (from Major Disadvantage to Minor Disadvantage, or from Minor Disadvantage to a standard roll). Targeting the Completely Restrained Character: The attack is made with a Major Advantage. The penalty for Called Shots is eliminated, and the attack is more likely to hit due to the target's vulnerability.

  3. Lock: Invoke an additional opposed Might Action Roll. On a success, the target gains an additional Tier of the Entangled Condition.

  4. Drag: Invoke an additional opposed Might Action Roll. Success here allows you to travel up to half of your Movement Speed while maintaining the grapple. This Action Roll is automatically successful on a Subdued target.

  5. Ranged Attack: While grappling, you can use a Major Action to make a Ranged Attack (not affected by Expertise or Expertise) against your target or another enemy within range. Targeting the Entangled Character: The attack is made with a Minor Advantage due to the close proximity. The penalty for Called Shots is reduced by one step (from Major Disadvantage to Minor Disadvantage, or from Minor Disadvantage to a standard roll). Targeting the Completely Restrained Character: The attack is made with a Major Advantage. The penalty for Called Shots is eliminated, and the attack is more likely to hit due to the target's vulnerability. Targeting Another Character: The attack is made with a Minor Disadvantage (-2) due to the difficulty of aiming accurately while engaged in close combat.

  6. Throw: Invoke an additional Might Action Roll. Success here throws your target a number of yards equal to half your Might score, dealing damage based on the AP value of whatever they hit.

Both you and your target suffer a -2 penalty to Defense while grappling. If the target is Helpless, they suffer a -5 penalty to Defense.

Each round, the Entangled character can attempt to break free or take control of the grapple by repeating the opposed Might Action Roll.

HEAL: You provide some quick first aid to an injured ally. You must be adjacent to your ally and you must have bandages ready. See the Healing section below for specific rules regarding Heal Action Rolls.

Failed Heal Tests

Failing a Heal Action Roll can unfortunately make a situation much worse. If the Action Roll is failed by 2 or more, the target of the action endures a Minor Complication, gaining a Condition appropriate to the first aid attempted. If the Action Roll is failed by 5 or more, the target of the action endures a Major Complication, losing an amount of Health equal to the Healing character's Chance Die, and gaining 2 Conditions appropriate to the first aid attempted. A failed Heal check can be retried, either until the target character is dead, or the Healing character succeeds, which then applies the Successful Heal Action Roll results.

Example

Theo moves to his wounded ally, pulling bandages from his medic's bag. His ally has been shot, and he's got to work fast to get the bullet out. Theo, unfortunately, gets a 9 total for his Heal Action Roll, failing the roll.

The GM decides, based on what he was attempting, that the most appropriate condition in this situation is a level of the Bleeding Condition.

Theo attempts a second time. This time, he rolls a 5, failing the roll exceptionally. His ally takes 3 unresisted Piercing Damage, another level of Bleeding, and Nauseated Conditions, as Theo was a bit too far off with his knife placement in attempting to extract the bullet. Taking a second to breathe, calming himself from the stress, Theo tries a third time, this time succeeding with a 14.

The ally gains the benefit of the specified Heal action, and can no longer benefit from another Heal Action Roll until taking additional damage or resting.

MELEE ATTACK: You attack one adjacent enemy (within 2 yards) with your fists, boots, or a hand. RANGED ATTACK: You fire or throw a missile weapon at one visible enemy within range.

Near Miss

In combat, some attacks may fall just short of hitting, but still have an effect on the target. If an attack roll misses, but is within 2 points of a character's Defense, the attack technically hits, but does the minimum amount of damage possible, to a minimum of 1."

SPRINT: You can move up to double your Speed in yards. You cannot take this action if you are prone (you’d need to use the Move action to stand up first).

docs.title: Attacks & Damage
docs.text:
Combat revolves around two primary attack types: Melee and Ranged, both resolved the same way:

  1. Choose a Target. Melee attacks require you to be adjacent (within 2 yards) of an opponent. Ranged attacks require line of sight and must be within your weapon's range.
  2. Roll to Attack. Make an Action Roll using the appropriate Ability for your weapon (e.g., Might for Archaic Melee, Perception for Firearms), modified by Traits, Expertise, and/or other relevant bonuses.
  3. Compare Results. Your attack roll must meet or exceed the target's Defense to hit.
  4. Calculate Base Damage. On a successful hit, you deal an amount of Damage equal to Weapon Damage + Ability Score. Chance Damage (On Doubles). Whenever you roll Doubles on an attack, add your Chance Die to your Base Damage. Critical Damage (on Triples). Whenever you roll Triples, add twice your Chance Die to your Base Damage.

docs.title: Death & Dying
docs.text:
• When a PC's Health reaches 0, they begin dying, losing consciousness and perishing in 2 + Constitution in rounds unless healed. They can speak on their turn, but take no actions.

  • Most NPCs die instantly at 0 Health, though GMs can allow PC rules for significant foes at their discretion.

  • Health does not drop below 0 --- there are no negative Health values.

Pulling Killing Blows

  • If an attacker wishes to incapacitate instead of kill with an attack that would leave a target at 0 Health, they can declare a non-lethal attack. For any weapon that does not deal bludgeoning damage, this attack is made at a Major Disadvantage (-5). A successful attack leaves the target at 1 Health, regardless of damage total, and the target suffers Stunned (T5) for 1 minute (4 rounds) or until healed.

Delivering a Coup de Grace

  • A Helpless, Unconscious, or Dying character can be executed as a Major Action. The attacker forces a special Saving Throw (TN 10 + Base Damage).

  • If the target is Helpless or Unconscious but not already Dying, failure reduces them to 0 Health and they begin Dying. Success leaves them at 1 Health but Unconscious for a duration rolled on the Downtime Chart, regardless of healing.

  • If the target is already Dying, they automatically fail and are killed outright.

docs.title: Healing
docs.text:
Conversely, when a character is wounded, there are several ways to be healed.

Immediate Care: Another character can render immediate aid as a Heal action, as described previously in this chapter. The attempting character must roll a Healing (Wits) Action Roll CR 10. Immediate Care must be applied within 1 minute (4 Rounds) of an injury occurring to be effective. You can only benefit from Immediate Care once per Rest. A successful roll on Immediate Care recovers your Constitution in Health. First-Aid Healing: You or another character can aid you with a Heal action, as described previously in this chapter. You can only benefit from First Aid twice per Rest. The attempting Character must roll a Healing (Wits) Action Roll CR 12. A successful First-Aid Healing roll recovers an amount of Health equal to the attempting character's Level. Surgery: You or another character can aid you with a Heal action, as described previously in this chapter. Note that attempting a Surgery test on yourself is done with a Major Disadvantage (-5) to the roll. The attempting character must roll a Healing (Wits) Action Roll CR 15. A successful Surgery roll adds the attempting character's Wits and your Constitution to your next Rest's recovery amount. Failure deals damage to the target equal to what the recovery would have been. Surgery is one of the only ways to heal certain Conditions. Breather: You can take a 5 to 10-minute break after a combat encounter. After a breather, you get back 2 + Constitution in Health. The benefits of a Breather only occur once after an encounter's end, and not at all if you ended the encounter at 0 Health. Rest: You take 6-10 hours of minimal activity, focusing on your own recovery. If you can get at least 6 hours of rest, you heal 5 + Constitution in Health. Professional Medical Care: Being cared for in a professional medical environment grants significant benefits to healing. Most notably, a +2 bonus to Health recovery for every Rest.
