docs.title: Chapter 7: The Armory
docs.text: Characters will need equipment to survive — ranging from mundane items to advanced experimental gear. This chapter discusses the tools, armor, and weapons you'll encounter throughout The Nomad Chronicles.

docs.title: Common Items
docs.text:
Common modern items like smartphones, shoes, and jackets are considered a part of everyday life and typically don't require tracking unless they're relevant to a particular scenario. If characters are in an extraordinary survival situation or a heavily restricted zone, GMs may rule to limit or remove access to Common Items as the narrative requires.

Likewise, it's not exactly necessary to keep up with a lot of common consumables, such as gasoline, scraps, and food; except when characters aren't in a place with easy access to those items.

docs.title: Requisition Power
docs.text:
By virtue of profession, or by completing objectives and missions, your character is awarded with finances, contacts, and influence. Requisition Power reflects a combination of those factors.

Instead of tracking exact currency values, equipment in this game is acquired using Requisition Power (RP), a measure of your character's influence, connections, and resources. RP is gained through Background, Mission Rewards, and Downtime Work.

RP Examples Example 1: Dash needs to capture and contain a high-value target. He needs a rifle, ammunition, and a set of handcuffs. These items should be provided by the organization assigning him. His RP could represent his rank and approved paperwork turned into his superiors, pursuant to his mission. Example 2: Theo needs to collect samples from a nearby biological weapons facility. But, as this is a personal project, he has to provide the materials himself. Using RP in this case could represent connecting phone calls, licensing fees, and/or purchasing bits and pieces of kit from a local vendor.

At the start of the game, and upon leveling up, players roll 2d6 and add their Background's RP Bonus. If doubles are rolled, the Chance Die is rolled, and the result is added to the RP total, before any further calculation, such as from the Leadership Expertise (See Expertise). Players are guaranteed a minimum amount equal to their Background's RP Bonus regardless of the roll.

Players also gain this RP bonus at the beginning of every in-game month, as long as they use at least some downtime to perform in their particular background field. Players enrolled in a program like the Nomad Initiative are provided Military Background RP as their monthly pay, regardless of their personal background.

GMs are encouraged to provide RP rewards as part of the game. Most often, this is going to involve payments and an increase in influence for completing missions or assignments.

Mission Payments

2d6 x Threat Level

  • GMs seeking to make adjustments to this amount should consult Chapter 8: GameMaster's Notes.

RP can be used during downtime to purchase gear, vehicles, weapons, etc. At the end of every in-game month, an amount of RP is deducted for living expenses and any leased or rented items.

docs.title: Active Inventory
docs.text:
A person can only carry so much. A character's carry capacity is determined by their Might Ability.

Active Inventory refers to the amount of gear a character can store on their person in a ready, mission-accessible fashion. Inventory is measured in Units, where each Unit represents a manageable amount of space and weight.

If a character carries more Units than their Inventory Capacity allows, they become encumbered. For every 1 Unit (or every 25 lbs, if using weight tracking) beyond the limit, the character gains 1 tier of Fatigue per minute of sustained movement or activity.

Encumbrance is cumulative and dangerous, especially in hostile environments. Excess gear should be stowed or managed with caution.

docs.title: Untrained? Why Wait?
docs.text:
As described in Chapter 4, some equipment requires formal training. This applies to Weapons, Armor, and other specialized equipment. Characters that are considered untrained gain only limited benefit from the item in question.

Untrained with Armor: Gain only half of the item's Defense and Armor Protection (AP). Cannot apply bonuses granted by Constitution through Armor Training Traits. Untrained with Weapons:

May only make attacks using the appropriate Ability Score (Might, Reflexes, or Perception).

Do not add an Ability Score to base damage.

May only benefit from circumstantial bonuses (e.g., flanking, elevation, surprise).

Training is the key to mastery. Without it, survival becomes that much harder.

docs.title: Living Expenses & Lifestyles
docs.text:
A character's lifestyle affects their narrative comfort, social standing, and access to basic items. Higher lifestyles offer assumed perks and access to quality-of-life items, while lower ones may limit options.

Lifestyle Monthly Cost Squalid: 0 (Free) Impoverished: 10 RP Modest: 20 RP Comfortable: 30 RP Wealthy: 40 RP Aristocratic: 50 RP

docs.title: Squalid
docs.text:
Little to no expenses. Characters in this lifestyle are often homeless or living on scraps with bare-bones living. Likely sharing rooms, eating the cheapest possible food, and have minimal liquid resources for luxuries, such as electronic devices and transportation.

Note

This is the only lifestyle in which the Common Items discussed at the beginning of this Chapter are not guaranteed. GMs are encouraged to price these items appropriately, so that if a character is able to save up, they'll have opportunity to begin buying these basic items.

docs.title: Impoverished
docs.text: A slight step up in quality of life. Likely to still be sharing an apartment or townhouse, but still struggling to get by and relying on public transportation.

docs.title: Modest
docs.text: The average working adult, able to afford a furnished apartment, decent food, and some level of leisure or convenience, such as a car or entertainment devices.

docs.title: Comfortable
docs.text: More disposable income. A PC here would likely have a better home to live in, such as a small house or luxury apartment. They likely have luxuries such as a better car or travel options, and more expensive tastes in food and entertainment.

docs.title: Wealthy
docs.text: High-end living, premium real estate, multiple vehicles, fine dining, vacations, etc.

docs.title: Aristocratic
docs.text:
Mansions, private planes, hired help, luxury everywhere. These are extremely high-end consumers, and the sky is the limit on cost.

Warning

If a player isn't careful, they can overextend themselves, financially. The guidelines in this note are only applicable if the campaign goes on with enough time to justify it. PCs are paid at the beginning of each in-game month, and pay for their Living Expenses at the end of the month.

Paul has lived a Comfortable Lifestyle for a while, but occasionally has a bit of an overspending habit. He decides that, for his birthday, he's going to buy and modify an upgraded rifle. He ends up spending far too much, leaving only 50 RP for living expenses. At the end of the month, his Comfortable Lifestyle is downgraded to modest living conditions. Several leased or rented items are repossessed, but it's unfortunately not enough to cover the difference. Either way, the financial strain is evident, and can potentially become a larger problem if left unchecked.

docs.title: Expensive Items
docs.text:
Some items simply can't be bought or requisitioned outright, without the right amount of money and/or connections. These items often come with payment plans, or leases, in which the customer pays a monthly amount to (eventually) own the item.

Any item of 30 or higher RP, at the GM's discretion, can possibly be broken down into monthly payments of RP, the amount of which is decided on at the time of purchase.

Info

Example: A Forced Entry Unit armor costs 583 RP (representing ~$17,490 or so). A character wishing to own/use this item could make an agreement for 6.5-7 RP/month for 90 months, or 49 RP/month for 1 year.

docs.title: Bundles & Packs
docs.text: For when you want to get right into the game, or decide you don't feel like comparing items across multiple tables, these pre-packed inventories are here for your convenience.

docs.title: Basic Kit
docs.text:
• Basic Pack (5 RP): Canteen, Leather Jacket, Shiv (Set of keys, pencil, pen, etc)

  • Gang Novice Pack (15 RP): Concealed Knife or Brass Knuckles, Leather Jacket, Revolver

  • Infiltration Pack (20 RP): Locksmith's Kit, Flashlight, Taser Pistol

  • Concealed Carry Pack (12 RP): Compact Pistol, Holster (Concealed), 1 Reload Box

  • Minor Emergency Pack (6 RP): First-Aid Kit, Flashlight, Handheld Taser

docs.title: Standard Kit
docs.text:
• Athlete's Pack: Canteen, Food (4), Running Shoes

  • Blue Collar Pack: 1 Tool Kit (related to profession), 1 Unit of Crafting Material, Waist Bag

  • Law Enforcement Pack: Light Pistol with 1 Reload Box, Handcuffs, Gun Belt

  • Medicine Pack: Medic's Bag,

  • Military Pack: Tactical Knife, Holster (Tactical), 1 Unit MRE (12)

  • Scholar Pack: Backpack, Reference Manual, 1 Tool Kit

  • White Collar Pack: Stylish Outfit (+5 to social checks), Membership Card (treated as having +5 RP when purchasing Vehicles or Miscellaneous Gear), Emergency Contact (1 Chosen Connection)

docs.title: Specialist Kit
docs.text:
• Assassin's Pack (50 RP): Stiletto, Custom Pistol, Suppressor, 3 Reload Boxes

  • Assaulter's Pack (70 RP): Submachine Gun, Assault Rifle, 6 Reload Boxes, 2 Grenade (Fragmentation)

  • Breacher's Pack (75 RP): C-4 (1 pound), Shotgun, 6 Reload Boxes, Submachine Gun

  • Marksman's Pack (80 RP): Machine Pistol, Marksman Rifle, 6 Reload Boxes

  • Field Medic's Pack: Portable Hospital, Painkillers, Adrenaline Booster

  • Researcher's Pack (50 RP): Research Tablet, 1 Tool Kit, 1 Unit Sample Tubes (5)

  • Sniper's Pack (70 RP): Sniper Rifle, Revolver, 6 Reload Boxes

[Review Necessary, Expansion Ideas?]

docs.title: Armor
docs.text:
Armor provides additional protection from incoming damage. It is known by the term Armor Protection, or AP. AP is used to determine how much damage gets through to your body, how heavy the armor is, and how it affects your movement speed.

Much like weapons, covered later in this chapter, armor has been mostly abstracted for ease of play. Modifying to represent specific brands or armor systems should be discussed with your GM.

Armor Training: Like with weapons, a character must be familiar with their armor in order to get the most out of using it. Armor training is tracked in levels similarly to an Expertise. Each level indicates how much of your Constitution can be applied as a bonus to your Armor Protection (AP). Armor Training Levels Constitution Bonus Basic +1/2 Con Initiate +Con Advanced +Con +1 Expert +Con +2

Defense & Speed

Defense How hard it is to get hit. Determined by 9 + Reflexes + Armor Defense Bonus. Armor Defense Bonus: Your Armor Defense Bonus is determined by your armor's weight. Light Armor grants a +2 Bonus, Medium Armor grants a +3 Bonus, and Heavy Armor grants a +5 Bonus. Armor Protection: How hard it is to get hurt from being hit. Determined by Constitution + AP Speed: How fast your character moves in a turn of combat. Speed is determined by your Reflexes ability, and modified by your Armor Protection. Starting at 0, a character can move 10 yards per turn, increasing or decreasing that movement speed by 2 yards per turn. A character with 0 Speed cannot move, and automatically fails Reflex Saving Throws.

Wearing armor provides a static penalty (based on the AP of the armor) to your Ability for determining speed. For example, a police officer with a Reflexes of +2 can normally move at 14 yards per turn. Wearing a Light-Duty Vest (AP 5), however, makes his Reflexes count as a -3 for movement purposes, making him move at 4 yards per turn due to the encumbrance of the armor.

A lot of the armor and weapons are labeled somewhat generically, and given the properties they are likely to have on average.

If you or your GM would like to be even more specific, however, feel free to discuss and alter things; for, as we all know, no two pistol or rifle brands are the exact same. If more options are necessary for your table to have fun, put properties together to make more specific weapons. GMs are encouraged to have an idea for adjusting the cost of their weapons to reflect said specificity.

docs.title: Light Armor
docs.text: Armor Defense: +2 Units: 3 Armor AP Min. Might Cost Notes Light Armor Leather Jacket 2 0 5 - Civilian/Covert Armor 4 1 7 - Light Undercover Shirt 5 1 15 - Undercover Vest 6 2 20 -

docs.title: Leather Jacket
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +2, Cost: 5 RP

A leather jacket, possibly with sewn-in decoration. Provides a Minor Advantage on Saving Throws against the Hypothermic Condition. Provides an additional +1 bonus to Defense against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut. Does not count as armor for the purposes of AP or HP rounds.

docs.title: Civilian/Covert Armor
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +2, Cost: 10 RP

A light, discreet set of armor that offers some protection without looking like military or tactical gear. Provides a +1 bonus to social checks involving diplomacy. Emphasizes mobility over protection.

docs.title: Light Undercover Shirt
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +5, Cost: 15 RP

A shirt made of bullet resistant material. The fibers are woven to ensure even dispersal of energy when a bullet strikes it. Usually rated against Pistol caliber weapons. Provides an additional +3 bonus to AP against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Undercover Vest
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +6, Cost: 20 RP

A Kevlar or ceramic vest designed to fit underneath the clothing for concealment, to stop most pistol caliber rounds, and some shotgun rounds. Provides an additional +3 bonus to AP against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Armored Suit
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +2, Cost: 45 RP

A dress suit made of bullet and cut resistant fabrics. Often utilized by groups such as the US Secret Service, in the protection details of high-value individuals. It provides reasonable enough protection at a minimum cost to mobility. The downside to this is that it cannot be modified, outside of stylistic choices.

docs.title: Medium Armor
docs.text: Armor Defense: +3 Units: 6 Armor AP Min. Might Cost Notes Medium Armor Concealable Vest 5 1 10 - Light-Duty Vest 6 2 20 - Tactical Vest 8 2 25 -

docs.title: Concealable Vest
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +6, Cost: 25 RP

Similar to the Undercover Vest, but with one key difference, being that it has pouches designed to hold replaceable armor plates. Provides an additional +3 bonus to Defense against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Light Duty Vest
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +6, Cost: 30 RP

A light Kevlar vest worn on the outside of clothing. Often standard-issue for most police departments. Provides an additional +3 bonus to Defense against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Tactical Vest
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +7, Cost: 40 RP

A more robust armor system, utilizing replaceable armor plates. This armor is often standard-issue for tactical police units, and military units. Has +4 Attachment Points for additional equipment to be mounted to it. Provides an additional +3 bonus to Defense against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Heavy Armor
docs.text: Armor Defense: +5 Units: 9 Armor AP Min. Might Cost Notes Heavy Armor Hazard Protection Armor 8 3 30 - Special Response Vest 9 3 40 - Forced Entry Unit 12 4 583 (40/month) -

docs.title: Hazard Protection Armor
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +8, Cost: 45 RP

A full-body suit of armor that hosts a number of features designed to keep the wearer safe against all manner of contaminants, radiation, lack of atmosphere, environmental hazards such as extreme temperatures.

Built-in environmental seals and self-cleaning filters make this armor a must-have item for nuclear, biological, and chemical warfare.

Hazard Protection Armor must be doffed and filters must be allowed a cleaning cycle at least once every 8 hours of use to continue functioning properly.

docs.title: Special Response Vest
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +9, Cost: 50 RP

This armor is made to carry removable plates rated up to high powered rifle rounds. It's been hardened to provide maximum defense by sacrificing mobility. Provides an additional +3 bonus to Defense against Called Shots aimed at the Chest, Heart, or Vitals/Gut.

docs.title: Forced Entry Unit
docs.text:
Armor Protection: +12, Cost: 70 RP

A bulky, heavy armor kit designed for dealing with heavy weapons and explosive devices. Hardened. Provides an additional +4 Armor Protection against damage from all explosive devices. Provides an additional +3 bonus to Defense against all Called Shots.

docs.title: Shields
docs.text:
Although not in the same demand as they used to be, shields do still find their way into modern tactical discussion. They provide a mobile barrier, for improved safety during high-risk situations.

Shields and AP

Shields give only half their AP as a penalty to Reflexes for determining Speed.

Shield Defense AP Cost Notes Riot Shield +2 +4 15 - Tower Shield +4 +8 30 Heavy

docs.title: Weapons
docs.text: Sometimes, words just won't do. When things go bad, you have to pick up a weapon, and get moving. Look for cover, and don't get pinned in.

docs.title: Weapon Groups
docs.text: Weapons in this system are broken into groups. Training and Proficiency with weapon groups is simple. Before you can take the Trait for a bonus to using a certain Weapon Group, you must first be trained in its use. This can be done by spending SP. Whenever you gain training in a weapon group, mark that group on your Character Sheet. As with armor, the weapons listed are mostly generalized and broad. If more specificity is needed, players and GMs should coordinate, and the GM should adjust the RP cost accordingly. That said, the weapon groups and their descriptions are as follows:

docs.title: Melee Groups
docs.text: Archaic Melee: Weapons in this category aren't as common in a modern setting, but there are some in the world who still train in their use. This category includes weapons such as: Swords, Spears, and Axes. Brawling: Weapons in this category are generally items that are worn on or around the hand. This category includes weapons such as: Brass Knuckles, Boxing Gloves, Tactical Gloves, and Hand Wraps. note: Unarmed Attacks, while not technically an item, had to be detailed somewhere. Light Melee: Light Melee weapons are versatile, meaning they can be used with either Might or Reflexes, and can be used with a single hand, leaving the other free to perform other tasks, such as rude gestures. Most small martial arts weapons are found in this category, such as: Batons, Knives, and Tasers. Heavy Melee: Heavy Melee weapons are often large and bulky, or require specialized martial training to be utilized. They often require both hands to use properly. Weapons in this category can include: Quarterstaves, Machetes, and Sledgehammers. Improvised Melee: If it can be lifted and swung, you can likely hit someone with it. These items are likely to be unwieldy or awkward to fight with. The damage these items cause is based on the material they're made from. Add the AP value of the item's material to the damage dice of this weapon. Light Melee Weapons thrown would also be counted in this category.

docs.title: Ranged Groups
docs.text: Archaic Ranged: Ranged weapons from a bygone age, now seen mostly for their sport or hunting potential. These are weapons such as: Bows and Crossbows Small-Arms: Small-Arms can be one or two-handed firearms, but typically feature a lighter frame, and smaller caliber ammunition. Small-Arms are weapons such as: Light Pistols, Submachine Guns, and Heavy Pistols. Long-Arms: These firearms are longer, larger, and typically fire heavier or more specialized rounds. Weapons in this group are those such as: Rifles and Shotguns. Heavy-Arms: These weapons tend to be particularly heavy, bulky, or unwieldy; often requiring more specialized training to properly implement in combat. Weapons in this group are those such as: Machine Guns, Grenade Launchers, and Rocket Launchers. Improvised Ranged: If it can somehow launch a projectile, you know which end to point at an enemy. These are very unusual items, not normally used for the purposes of a standard firearm. The damage caused by these weapons is based on the material the projectile is made from. Add the AP value of the item's material to the damage dice of this weapon.

docs.title: Other Groups
docs.text: Thrown Weapons: These weapons are designed to be thrown to cause damage. A lot of them are either heavy, bladed, or otherwise sufficiently dangerous, but difficult to fire out of any kind of launcher. This could include: Javelins, Throwing Knives, etc. Explosives: Not necessarily a weapon group used in direct contention, although they could be. Explosives are detailed further below, but tend to be those items used in the destruction of materiel.

docs.title: Archaic Melee Weapons
docs.text:
Most Weapons in this group add your Might Ability to the Damage indicated, after accounting for the Chance Die.

docs.title: Sap
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning Damage (Stun)

This cloth-wrapped club is designed for stealthy, non-lethal takedowns. Illegal to own in most jurisdictions.

docs.title: Mace
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning Damage (Armor-Piercing)

A classic blunt weapon with a heavy sphere atop a short, strong handle. A simple weapon, historically used effectively against armored opponents. If using a spiked variant, damage changes to Piercing.

docs.title: Quarterstaff
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Bludgeoning Damage (Reach, Versatile, Double)

A 3-yard pole, usually made of wood. More modern designs tend to be made of stronger, lighter weight materials, such as carbon fiber.

docs.title: Warhammer
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Bludgeoning Damage (Versatile, Armor-Piercing, Knockdown)

A handheld hammer with a large blunt face designed to crush heavy armor.

docs.title: Dagger
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Piercing Damage (Finesse, Thrown, Precise, Armor-Piercing)

A 5 to 7 inch straight blade, designed to be stabbed into the gaps between plate armor.

docs.title: Spear
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Piercing/2 Bludgeoning (Reach, Versatile, Thrown, Double)

A 2-yard pole, usually made of wood, with a sharpened, bladed, or barbed tip on one end. Can be used non-lethally by treating the back half of the weapon as a quarterstaff. Doing so deals Bludgeoning damage as indicated.

docs.title: Rapier
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Piercing (Finesse, Precise)

A weapon that's seen far more use in the modern age as a martial sport, the rapier is a thin, light weapon designed to prioritize speed and precision over brute strength.

docs.title: Short Sword
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing/Piercing (Finesse, Bleed)

An 18-inch, razor-sharp double-edged blade often utilized in close combat.

docs.title: Battle Axe
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing/Piercing (Versatile, Armor-Piercing)

A handheld cleaving weapon designed to defeat armor. A 2-yard pole topped with a 1-foot-long curved blade on one end of the handle. The opposite side of the curved blade features a tapered spike.

docs.title: Longsword
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing/Piercing

Also historically termed an "arming sword," it is typically a 25 to 30-inch straight, double-edged slashing weapon with a cross-shaped hilt.

docs.title: Halberd
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing/Piercing (Reach, Two-Handed, Armor-Piercing)

The best aspects of the axe and spear are combined in this lengthy polearm, offering a heavy weapon designed to use momentum to cleave through armored opponents with ease.

docs.title: Bastard Sword
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing (Reach, Versatile)

Often called a "hand-and-a-half" longsword or a "claymore", this bladed weapon was designed with versatility in mind. It is a longsword with a slightly longer, heavier blade, and enough room on the hilt to comfortably employ a second hand in its use.

docs.title: Greatsword
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing (Reach, Two-Handed, Armor-Piercing)

These much larger swords sometimes reached 3 or 4 yards in length, designed to combat armored, horse-mounted opponents.

docs.title: Brawling Weapons
docs.text:
All Weapons in this group may add your Might or Reflexes Ability to the Damage indicated.

Weapon Damage Min. Might Cost Units Notes Fist 2 - - - - Boxing Gloves 2 1 6 1 Versatile, Bleed, Two-Handed Brass Knuckles 2 1 5 1 Versatile, Stun Hand Wraps 2 0 2 - Stun Stun Baton 3 0 15 2 Ionic or Bludgeoning Damage Tactical Gloves 3 0 10 - - Improvised Weapon 2 0 0 1-3 Variable Damage

docs.title: Fist
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning

This is your basic Unarmed Damage. A bare knuckle, a boot, or even a headbutt should the need arise.

docs.title: Boxing Gloves
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning (Versatile, Bleed, Two-Handed)

These gloves both protect the hands from damage, and make for an effective non-lethal solution to conflicts.

docs.title: Brass Knuckles
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning (Versatile, Stun)

Brass Knuckles are illegal in most places, but not necessarily hard to come by. It is a single piece of metal designed to protect the hand and deliver more potent damage than a naked fist.

docs.title: Hand Wraps
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning (Stun)

These wraps compress the hands to keep them protected during unarmed combat, while allowing for an increased focus on striking power.

docs.title: Stun Baton
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Ionic Damage or 2 Bludgeoning Damage (if unpowered)

A 6-8 inch steel rod with a rubberized handle. Good for stunning targets. Houses a rechargeable battery, good for up to 20 uses before it needs recharging. Also a favored tool of certain groups to use as an improvised sap.

docs.title: Tactical Gloves
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Bludgeoning

A pair of ceramic-armored leather gloves, designed to protect the hands from damage, as well as to assist in the performance of mission tasks. Additionally, provides a Minor Advantage on checks involving the extended application of physical strength.

docs.title: Light / Stealth Melee Weapons
docs.text:
Weapons in this group can often double as Stealth or Thrown Weapons, though that may not be their primary purpose. These weapons are built with maneuverability and precision in mind, and can be used with either your Might or Reflexes Ability.

Weapon Damage Min. Might Cost Units Notes Shiv 2 0 0 1 Concealable, Improvised, Bleed Concealed Knife 2 0 5 1 Finesse, Concealable Karambit 2 2 12 1 Finesse, Concealable, Bleed Stiletto 2 0 8 2 Concealable, Precision, Armor-Piercing Tactical Knife 4 1 10 2 Finesse,Thrown,Leverage Handheld Taser 1 0 5 2 Ionic, Concealable

docs.title: Shiv
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Piercing (Concealable, Improvised, Bleed)

A makeshift stabbing weapon. Deadly in the right ( or wrong) hands.

docs.title: Concealed Knife
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Slashing/Piercing (Concealable, Finesse)

A small, easily concealed bladed weapon. It could be hidden in a boot, under clothing, or even in a pocket.

docs.title: Karambit
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Slashing (Finesse, Concealable, Bleed)

A small, curved knife designed for devastating slashing attacks.

docs.title: Stiletto
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Piercing (Concealable, Precision, Armor-Piercing)

A successor to the archaic dagger, the Stiletto is a thin, pointed blade designed for quick, lethal strikes to the unarmored portions of armored opponents.

docs.title: Tactical Knife
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing/Piercing (Finesse, Thrown, Leverage)

A 6-8 inch fixed-blade knife, designed with combat in mind, but also useful in other, more mundane applications.

docs.title: Handheld Taser
docs.text:
Damage: 1 Ionic (Concealable)

A small battery-operated box fitted with conductive prongs on one end. Designed to complete a circuit and deliver a strong shock when pressed against an organic target. Can not be used to target objects. Chance to cause Stun and Paralysis.

docs.title: Heavy/Martial Melee Weapons
docs.text:
Weapons of this category tend to be large, awkward to wield, or require specific training to use properly.

Might Cost Units Notes Climbing Hook 2 3 12 2 Improvised,Bleed,Thrown, Versatile Crowbar 3 2 10 3 Improvised, Leverage Baseball Bat 3 1 4 2 Stun Combat Hatchet 3 1 8 2 Thrown Machete 3 2 12 2 Bleed Katana 4 2 15 2 Versatile, Bleed Fireman's Axe 4 3 20 2 Versatile, Armor-Piercing Sledgehammer 5 4 10 3 Two-Handed, Knockdown

docs.title: Climbing Hook
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Piercing (Improvised, Bleed, Thrown, Versatile)

A fairly common, specialized tool for arctic explorers and mountain climbers, this sharp hook is designed to assist in climbing otherwise unclimbable surfaces made of stone or ice. This design, however, also makes it rather dangerous in the hands of someone who knows how to swing it. If used for its intended purposes, grants a Major Advantage (+5) to climbing checks.

docs.title: Crowbar
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Bludgeoning (Improvised, Leverage)

A versatile tool that can also serve as a weapon. Useful in both exploration and combat scenarios, it serves something as a bridge between them. When used to pry open or break part of an object, gain a Major Advantage (+5) bonus to the check. note: these statistics could alternatively be used to describe a tire-iron.

docs.title: Baseball Bat
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Bludgeoning (Stun)

A 1-yard, tapered length of wood, aluminum, or carbon fiber designed to be swung with force.

docs.title: Combat Hatchet
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing (Thrown)

A handheld axe, compact and powerful weapon that can double as a thrown weapon.

docs.title: Machete
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing (Bleed)

Primarily a tool for plant clearing, but deadly as a weapon. The machete has found an unfortunate home in many groups of ill repute.

docs.title: Katana
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing (Versatile, Precise, Bleed)

A traditional weapon known for sharpness and precision, and designed to be flexibly used in close combat.

docs.title: Fireman's Axe
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Slashing (Versatile, Armor-Piercing)

A heavy, powerful tool designed for breaking through difficult barriers, which also makes it effective against armor.

docs.title: Sledgehammer
docs.text:
Damage: 5 Bludgeoning (Two-Handed, Knockdown, Armor-Piercing)

A heavy tool that delivers devastating force. Normally used to break walls and objects, this tool is capable of dealing significant damage to whatever it hits. If this weapon hits an object that is not being held or worn, the weapon's damage increases by 1, and the Armor-Piercing feature increases to a -5 penalty to object AP, as opposed to the normal -2 penalty.

Note

Versatile Weapons: Weapons labeled as Versatile can be used either one-handed or two-handed. If used with a two-handed grip, the weapon adds additional damage equal to the Minimum Might Requirement of the Weapon.

Two-Handed Weapons: Weapons labeled as Two-Handed Weapons require both hands to utilize properly. Using them with one hand imposes a Major Disadvantage (-5) on all Action Rolls made using the weapon.

docs.title: Improvised Melee Weapons
docs.text: For assault with a weapon no one thought could be deadly, but unfortunately was.

docs.title: Improvised Melee Weapon
docs.text:
Damage: 1 Variable (Improvised)

This can be just about any item that isn't nailed down that you can get your hands around and heft, an item that has been modified through crafting, or a firearm being utilized as a melee weapon for any reason. This weapon cannot be any larger than an equivalent weapon appropriate for wielding.

An Improvised weapon can make contact with a character or another object 10 times before it breaks partially, going down 1 AP threshold per break, as indicated in the Equipment: Damage & Destruction subsection. The Effects of the AP threshold only apply to the item's core function. A broken rifle probably ain't going to shoot right.

docs.title: Archaic Ranged Weapons
docs.text:
Weapons in this group have been used historically, some traditions continuing while the technology to create them has improved at an incredible rate.

Weapon Damage Ammo Min. Reflexes RP Cost Units Notes Blowgun 1 0 1 4 1 Silent Bowgun/Crossbow 4 1 2 20 2 Two-Handed, Armor-Piercing Compound Bow/Longbow 3 1 3 15 3 Heavy, Two-Handed Short Bow 2 1 2 10 2 Two-Handed, Fast Reload Sling 1 1 1 0 1 Silent, Cheap Ammo, Fast Reload

docs.title: Blowgun
docs.text:
Damage: 1 Piercing (Silent) Ammo: 1 Dart (Internal Magazine)

A primitive but highly effective stealth weapon, useful for non-lethal or lethal takedowns when paired with tranquilizer or poison darts.

docs.title: Bowgun/Crossbow
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Piercing (Two-Handed, Armor-Piercing) Ammo: 1 Arrow

As the technology to craft lighter and more reliable Crossbows have improved over the years, the training and use of them remains much the same. Modern crossbows tend to be constructed of fiberglass or carbon fiber.

docs.title: Compound Bow
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Piercing (Two-Handed, Precise) Ammo: 1 Arrow

Like crossbows, bows in general are still widely used. Both for sport shooting, and a staple weapon in hunting circles, the compound bow is a traditional weapon kept alive in the modern era.

docs.title: Short Bow
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Piercing (Two-Handed, Fast Reload, Precise) Ammo: 1 Arrow

The short bow was a staple of medieval warfare, allowing archers the ability to loose volleys of arrows at their opponents in a matter of moments.

Info

On Crossbows and Compound Bows: Rather than quibbling over minutiae in terms of traditional versus modern bows, the statistics presented here provide a generalized midpoint for combat capability. Should a GM or player choose to flavor their Compound Bow as, say, a traditional Longbow, it could be as simple as just saying that it's constructed of wood instead of modern materials.

Alternatively, should a GM or player require further specificity. (i.e.: an English Longbow was typically known for sacrificing ease of use for pure stopping power. Therefore, a GM could add the Heavy and Versatile Features to the weapon to indicate the amount of strength needed to loose an arrow from one, applying Might instead of Reflexes as a result.)

docs.title: Sling
docs.text:
Damage: 1 Bludgeoning (Silent, Cheap Ammo, Fast Reload) Ammo: 1 Object of Tiny size

A simple but deadly weapon used to hurl stones or projectiles with surprising force. Has the unique feature of Cheap Ammo, because a sling doesn't require any specialized ammunition. Any tiny object, such as a stone, can be used.

docs.title: Firearms
docs.text:
Listed below are several categories of firearms. There are Small-Arms, most of which are light and can be held and fired safely with one hand. Long-Arms, which are long bodied firearms such as rifles and shotguns, higher powered and useful for a variety of situations. And Heavy-Arms, weapons that have the potential to cause devastation, and are therefore usually restricted to military use.

There are a few special rules when dealing with firearms. As they are complex mechanical weapons, utilizing miniature explosions and moving parts, there are chances that something could go wrong with it.

Firearm Malfunctions

All firearms have a potential for Malfunction. Some more than others. Unless otherwise stated in the weapon's features, firearms will misfire on a roll of 4, and jam on a roll of 3.

Misfire: Rolling a 4 on 3d6, regardless of doubles, causes a misfire, where for some reason your weapon does not fire. This could be a stuck part on a revolver, or a double feed for a pistol. Misfires require a Minor Action to make a quick check and clear the weapon, making sure it's ready to fire again.

Jam: Rolling a 3 on 3d6, regardless of doubles, causes the weapon to jam. This could be a bullet casing lodged in the slide, or some other debris preventing the weapon from being fired. Jams require a Major Action to dislodge the obstruction and bring your weapon back into service.

Malfunction Roll Clear Action Misfire 4 Minor Action Jam 3 Major Action

Note: For a 6-cylinder revolver, increase RP cost by 5

A simple and sturdy weapon known for reliability and stopping power, despite usually having a lower rate of fire compared to other firearms.

docs.title: Compact Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Ballistic (Concealable, Quick Draw) Ammo: 9 Pistol Ammo

A smaller framed pistol that is easily concealable, making it a favorite of anyone needing a weapon that they can keep hidden in case of emergencies. This weapon comes equipped with a Safety Switch.

docs.title: Light Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Ballistic Ammo: 12 Pistol Ammo

A standard, simple firearm carried by many law enforcement officers, and military operatives around the world. This weapon comes equipped with a Safety Switch.

docs.title: Custom Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Ballistic (Precise, CP Boost) Ammo: 15 Pistol Ammo

This firearm has been custom tailored for its user, with a finely tuned action and slide, ergonomic grip, and responsive controls. This weapon comes equipped with a Trigger Safety.

docs.title: Machine Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Ballistic (Auto-Fire, Spray, Unreliable) Ammo: 30 Pistol Ammo

A fully-automatic pistol that sacrifices accuracy for rate of fire. This weapon comes equipped with a Safety Switch.

docs.title: Submachine Gun
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Ballistic (Select Fire) Ammo: 50 Pistol Ammo

Something of a bridge between pistols and rifles, Submachine Guns tend to be preferred for urban combat, due to a high rate of fire, combined with a low chance of overpenetration. Submachine Guns are capable of being set for Auto-Fire, Burst Fire, Semi-Auto, and Safety.

docs.title: Heavy Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 8 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, High Caliber) Ammo: 8 Magnum Ammo

A very powerful pistol known for its incredible stopping power.

docs.title: Taser Pistol
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Ionic (Single Shot) Ammo: 2 Taser Rounds

A two-use pistol primarily used for non-lethal incapacitation. Can be reloaded with a Taser Cartridge.

docs.title: Assault Shotgun
docs.text:
Damage: 7 Ballistic (Spread, Two-Handed, Armor-Piercing, Precise) Ammo: 12 Shotgun Shells or 12 Slugs

A highly versatile shotgun, with a fine-tuned barrel and choke for increased velocity and penetration. Makes it an incredible door opening device. This weapon is Semi-Auto, and comes fitted with a 12-Shell Box Magazine.

docs.title: Shotgun
docs.text:
Damage: 8 Ballistic (Spread, Two-Handed) Ammo: 8 Shotgun Shells

A classic Pump-Action Shotgun. Features Single Shot, an Internal Magazine capable of holding a respectable 8 shots.

docs.title: Assault Rifle
docs.text:
Damage: 7 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, Two-Handed) Ammo: 30 Rifle Ammo

A standard Assault Rifle, featuring several firing modes.

docs.title: Marksman Rifle
docs.text:
Damage: 8 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, Precise, Two-Handed) Ammo: 15 Rifle Ammo

A precision rifle, designed for counter-sniper operations and usually requisitioned to the Squad's Dedicated Marksman. It features Burst-Fire, Semi-Auto, and Safety Firing Modes.

docs.title: Sniper Rifle
docs.text:
Damage: 10 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, Precise, Two-Handed) Ammo: 8 Rifle Ammo

A precision rifle designed to deliver a devastating amount of damage across a long distance. This is a Single Shot, bolt-action rifle with a very heavy bullet.

docs.text:
Damage: 12 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, Precise, Two-Handed, Heavy, AP-Tuned, Reliable) Ammo: 4 Rifle Ammo

A devastating high caliber rifle, designed to destroy items and disable armored vehicles. This Single Shot weapon's bolt action feed system ensures this weapon can remain in service consistently.

docs.title: Grenade Launcher
docs.text:
Damage: N/A (Variable Ammo, Arc-Fire, Splash, Heavy, Two-Handed) Ammo: 1 Grenade

A Single Shot pneumatic launcher that can hurl a grenade in a longer arc than it could ordinarily be thrown. Including, but not limited to, over a wall — much to a covering target's dismay. For a 6-Cylinder variety (i.e.: a rotary launcher) of this weapon, the RP cost and Units is doubled, and it loses the ability to be used as an Underbarrel weapon.

docs.title: Machine Gun
docs.text:
Damage: 5 Ballistic (Armor-Piercing, Auto-Fire, Heavy, Spray, Two-Handed) Ammo: 50 Rifle Ammo

This weapon is meant for suppression and high-damage output during sustained firefights. Useful for crowd control, as it can be fitted with a number of different Magazine styles, or be fed by linkable belts, leaving the upper end of ammo amounts to seem as close to unlimited as it can get.

docs.title: RPG-7 Rocket-Propelled Grenade Launcher
docs.text:
Damage: 15 Blast Damage (Special Weapon, Precise, Armor-Piercing, AP-Tuned, Heavy, Two-Handed) Ammo: 1

This is a One-Time use weapon which, after being used, cannot be reloaded. Another RPG-7 must be purchased once used.

docs.title: C-4 (1 pound)
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Blast Radius: 2 yards per ounce Save: Reflexes CR 12 Special Properties: Shapeable, Stable, Destructive

Explodes in a radius of 2 yards per ounce. Can be reshaped to change the shape of the area of effect, using a Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 12. This reshaping cannot be attempted by any character without the Wits (Explosives) Expertise. The Damage of C-4 gains a +3 cumulative bonus for every pound added.

docs.title: Det-Cord (4 yards)
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Fire + 2 Blast Radius: N/A Save: N/A Special Properties: Shapeable, Stable

A replacement for cloth wicks, this cord resembles a yellow nylon rope. Within it is a stable blasting agent for use as an explosive, or to activate C-4 from a distance. Lengths of Det-Cord can be connected for more distance from the eventual explosion.

docs.title: Dynamite
docs.text:
Damage: 6 Blast (Unstable, Destructive) Radius: 4 yards Save: Reflexes CR 14 Special Properties: Unstable, Destructive

Explodes in a 4-yard radius. A staple explosive from years gone by, Dynamite tends to be prone to shock. If it's been wet or exposed to the natural elements for an extended period of time after being made, it can occasionally go off randomly without warning.

docs.title: Grenade (Concussion)
docs.text:
Damage: 2 Bludgeoning Radius: 6 yards Save: Constitution CR 13 Special Properties: Knockdown, Stun, Timed

Explodes in a 6-yard radius. A less lethal option, ideal for capturing and detaining, as opposed to outright elimination. Targets damaged by this weapon must make a Constitution Saving Throw CR 13. If failed, this weapon applies the Stunned and Prone Conditions.

docs.title: Grenade (Bean Bag)
docs.text:
Damage: 5 Bludgeoning Save: Constitution CR 12

Deals damage to a single target. The target must make a CR 12 Constitution Saving Throw or become Stunned.

docs.title: Grenade (Nitrogen)
docs.text:
Damage: 6 Cold Radius: 6 yards Save: Reflexes CR 14

Explodes in a 6 yard radius. Any creatures caught in the blast must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 15 or suffer the Hypothermic Condition.

docs.title: Grenade (Acid)
docs.text:
Damage: 6 Acid Radius: 5 yards

Explodes in a 5 yard radius. Any creatures caught in the splash of chemicals takes 5 Acid damage on the first round, then continues taking 3 Acid damage for the following 3 rounds.

docs.title: Grenade (Foam)
docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 6 yards Save: Might CR 15

Explodes in a 4 yard radius, foaming up then quickly hardening. Targets hit must make a Might Saving Throw CR 15 or gain the Entangled Condition.

docs.title: Grenade (Smoke)
docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 5 yard column Save: N/A Special Properties: Spread, Concealment, Color-Code

Releases opaque smoke in up to a 5-yard wide column for up to 1d6+1 minutes. Can be color-coded for tactical communications.

docs.title: Grenade (EMP)
docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 10 yard Save: Reflexes CR 10 Special Properties: Blind, Ion Blast, Timed

Explodes in a 10-yard radius. This radius goes through walls and other solid objects, disrupting electronics in its area. Targets that can see this weapon when it explodes must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 10 or be blinded until the end of their next turn.

docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 10 yards Save: Wits CR 14 Special Properties: Blind, Deafen, Timed

Explodes in a 10-yard radius. Targets within that range that can see and/or hear the explosion must make a Wits Saving Throw CR 14, or gain the Blinded and/or Deafened Conditions.

docs.title: Grenade (Fragmentation)
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Blast + 2 Slashing Radius: 6 yards Save: Reflexes CR 13 Special Properties: Bleed (T2), Timed

Explodes in a 6-yard radius. Targets damaged by this weapon must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 13. If successful, they take half damage. If they fail, this weapon applies two instances of the Bleeding Condition.

docs.title: Grenade (Improvised)
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Slashing + 2 Blast Radius: 4 yards Save: Reflexes CR 15 Special Properties: Bleed (T4), Unstable, Timed

A metal shell with a homemade explosive compound inside. This weapon explodes in a 4-yard radius. Targets damaged by this weapon must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 15. If successful, they take half damage. If they fail, this weapon applies 4 instances of the Bleeding Condition.

docs.title: Grenade (Teargas)
docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 4 yards + 1 yard per turn (max 10 yards) Save: Constitution CR 17 Special Properties: Spread, Nauseate, Suffocate

Starting at a 4-yard radius, the gas from this weapon spreads 1-yard per turn, up to 10 yards. This gas spreads through cracks and around corners to reach its full size. A sufficiently strong wind may dissipate it. Targets exposed to this gas must make a Constitution Saving Throw CR 17 or gain the Nauseated and Suffocation Conditions. If they leave the gas, the Suffocation Condition ends at the start of their following turn.

docs.title: Mine (Claymore)
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Blast + 3 Slashing Radius: 6 yard cone Save: Reflexes CR 15 Special Properties: Bleed (T5), Directional

Can be set with a Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 12. Can be disarmed with a Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 14. The front of this weapon explodes in a 6-yard cone. Targets damaged by this weapon must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 15. If successful, they take half damage. If they fail, this weapon applies 5 instances of the Bleeding Condition.

docs.title: Mine (EMP)
docs.text:
Damage: 0 Radius: 10 yard cone Save: Reflexes CR 10 Special Properties: Ion Blast

Can be set with a Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 12. Can be disarmed with a Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 14. The front of this weapon explodes in a 10-yard cone. This cone goes through walls and other solid objects, disrupting electronics in its area. Targets that can see this weapon when it explodes must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 10 or be Blinded until the end of their next turn.

docs.title: Molotov Cocktail
docs.text:
Damage: 3 Fire (Splash x2, Burn) Radius: 8 yards Save: Reflexes CR 12 Special Properties: Splash x2, Burn

Explodes in an 8-yard radius. Targets damaged by this weapon must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 12. If successful, they take half damage. If failed, this weapon applies the Burning Condition.

docs.title: Sticky Bomb
docs.text:
Damage: 4 Blast Radius: 4 yards Save: N/A Special Properties: Sticky, Destructive

Explodes in a 4-yard radius. One side of this device has a strong magnet. For applications where a metal surface is unavailable, there is a patch with a fast-acting strong adhesive. If the adhesive is used, this weapon can be applied to a target or surface with a successful Wits (Explosives) Action Roll CR 10.

docs.title: Thermite Charge
docs.text:
Damage: 10 Fire Radius: 6 yards Save: Reflexes CR 13 Special Properties: Sticky, Burn, Shapeable, Destructive

A 1-foot patch filled with thermite. When activated, it burns through structural and hardened materials, creating intense heat in a 6-yard radius. Anyone within 3 yards of this device while it's active must make a Reflexes Saving Throw CR 13. If successful, target takes half damage. If failed, this weapon applies the Burning Condition.

docs.title: Crafting
docs.text:
Over the course of a Nomad campaign, a character may or may not be exactly inclined to buy just any run-of-the-mill items. Perhaps they have an engineering background, and have an idea for a new device. Perhaps they simply want to forge their own weapons and armor. Perhaps they wish to concoct medicines or poisons which simply are not available over the counter.

Whatever the case may be, this Sub-Section of the Equipment Chapter is for those characters who can't leave an item half-finished.

Creating new items often costs time, money, and materials. How much time, how much money, and how much of what materials all depend on the kind of item you wish to create.

NOTE

When crafting, if something you're trying to build is out of the ordinary, check with your GM to consider the item's fit within the campaign setting, or power scaling.

docs.title: Core Crafting Mechanic
docs.text:
A character wishing to craft equipment must know something about what they're trying to build. This is represented by a Wits (Design) check with a CR based on the item's tier, as shown in the charts below. This Design check can take several hours to several days, again depending on the Crafting tier of the item in question.

The character may then roll a Crafting Action Roll with the appropriate Ability (Expertise) combination to the Craft Action Roll. Even if an item is to be crafted over several weeks, this check is only rolled once. On a failed design roll, the item is improperly designed, and cannot be crafted. On a failed craft roll the item is made, but broken beyond repair, and the materials to make it are lost.

If a character does not have the Expertise associated with the crafting check they are attempting, they roll the crafting check with a Major Disadvantage (-5).

Info

One exception to these mechanics is a character crafting a temporary item using Wits (Jury-Rigging). This special circumstance does not require a Design check, but the item crafted will always have complications associated with it. The rules surrounding this special circumstance will be detailed below.

In the charts below, you'll notice that the Crafting tiers have a range of CRs. This range represents the difficulty in crafting an item to perform exactly as you want it to. Your Design check only needs to exceed the lower CR to properly design an item. If your Craft check meets the lower CR or above, their item is made, but with a number of random complications equal to the character's Chance Die. Meeting the upper CR means the item will function exactly as designed. Finally, exceeding the upper CR will allow an item to not only function exactly as designed, but will also allow the item a number of bonuses equal to the character's Chance Die.

Basic Crafting is as follows:

  1. The primary crafter rolls their Wits (Design) test. This roll is compared to the Crafting chart for the CR and time spent.

  2. The primary crafter makes the initial crafting roll using their relevant Ability Score and relevant Expertise, if available, and is assumed to be working on their item for the time indicated. If this time is split in any way, they roll another craft check to pick up where they left off.

Crafting time can be reduced if multiple characters assist with the creation of an item. For each additional character actively involved in the crafting process, reduce the crafting time by 1 step, and add half of that character's relevant Ability Score (rounded down) as a bonus to the primary crafter's roll. The crafting time can only be reduced a maximum of 2 steps, regardless of the number of contributors.

Crafting Results:

  • Below Minimum CR: You fail to craft the item, and the resources used are consumed. For some standard items, this doesn't mean too much. For items such as poisons and explosives, however, this failure could potentially lead to harm. (See below)

  • Between Minimum and Maximum CR: You successfully craft the item you intended. It's not perfect, however, and is crafted with a number of randomly rolled Complications (See Complications & Bonuses Chart) equal to your Chance Die at the time of your roll.

  • Meeting Maximum CR: You successfully craft the item you envisioned, perfectly. The item now exists, and can be used until its used up, discarded or broken.

  • Above Maximum CR: You successfully craft the item you envisioned, but with a few last minute tweaks. If you exceed the Maximum CR by 2, you may choose a number of Bonuses from the Complications & Bonuses Chart equal to half the value of your Chance Die (Rounded Down), to a minimum of 1 Bonus. If you exceed the Maximum CR by 5, you may choose any number of Bonuses to add, up to the full value of your Chance Die.

Base Crafting Chart CR TierMin. CRMax. CRCraft TimeJury-Rig 1. Trivial5102-6 hours1 Minute 2. Common10151 day5 Minutes 3. Uncommon15201 Week15 Minutes 4. Rare20252 Weeks30 Minutes 5. Legendary25304 Weeks2 Hours

docs.title: Complications (3d6 Roll)
docs.text: Roll Complication 3 Item breaks on first use, dealing damage based on tier (1 for Trivial or Common Items, 2 for Uncommon Items, 3 for Rare Items, and 4 for Legendary Items). 4 Item's AP is considered 5 points lower, making it easier to break. 5 Item's effectiveness is reduced by 1 tier (e.g., a Rare item functions as an Uncommon item). 6 Item has a minor defect, reducing its usability (e.g., -1 to relevant checks or attacks). 7 Item is bulky, increasing its weight by 75%, imposing its AP times 3 to the wielder's Speed. 8 Item's lifespan is halved. 9 Item requires frequent maintenance, doubling maintenance time or cost. 10 Item is prone to malfunction, with a 10% chance to fail on use. 11 Item's weight is twice its AP, and heavier than expected. The wielder takes an additional penalty to Speed while this item is on their person. 12 Item is difficult to use, imposing a -2 penalty on relevant checks. 13 Item has a 10% chance to break under stress (e.g., during combat). 14 Item has a negative side effect when used (e.g., inflicts 1d6 damage to the user). 15 Item is incompatible with one type of equipment (e.g., cannot be used with specific armor). 16 Item has a minor design flaw, reducing the number of Modification Slots by 1. 17 Item has an adverse effect (e.g., imposes a -2 penalty to a random ability score while equipped). 18 Item causes unintended harm or danger (e.g., explodes dealing tier-based damage to any creatures within 3 yards).

docs.title: Bonuses (3d6 Roll)
docs.text:
Roll Bonus 3 Item grants a bonus equivalent to its tier on relevant checks or attacks (+1 for Trivial, +2 for Common, etc.). 4 Item's AP is considered 5 points higher, making it harder to break. 5 Item provides a +1 bonus on relevant checks. 6 Item grants a minor ability enhancement (+1 to a relevant ability score while equipped). 7 Item is exceptionally well-crafted, reducing maintenance time or cost by 50%. 8 Item has a pleasing aesthetic quality, providing a +1 bonus to social checks involving the item. 9 Item is lighter and easier to handle, reducing its weight by 25%. Reduce AP penalty to Speed by 1. 10 Item's effectiveness is increased by 1 tier (e.g., a Common item functions as an Uncommon item). 11 Item grants an extra minor benefit (e.g., +2 to a single relevant check per day). 12 Item has a unique design feature, providing a +1 bonus to a specific use. 13 Item has enhanced performance, granting a +2 bonus to relevant checks or attacks. 14 Item has an additional useful feature (e.g., may gain 1 bonus Modification). 15 Item grants a significant bonus (+2 to a relevant ability score while equipped). 16 Item is Masterfully crafted, providing a +3 bonus to relevant checks or attacks. 17 Item grants a unique ability or property (e.g., allows the user to perform a specific action once per day). 18 Item is of legendary quality, granting a +4 bonus to relevant checks or attacks and an additional unique property.

Below are several examples of items and substances you can craft. These lists are not intended to be all-inclusive, but could potentially be a jumping off point for those focused on crafting.

docs.text: Someone with the requisite skills can arm themselves rather effectively while keeping costs down. docs.title: Acids & Bases
docs.text: Acids are damaging, corrosive substances. Bases can be used to neutralize acids or vice versa. Counteracting damage from a base or acid requires a use of its opposite at the same potency. Using a lesser potency simply reduces the damage caused by the substance in question. In the potency column, the numbers in parentheses are typical splash and/or immersion damage caused per round of exposure to the caustic substance. Further information about Acid Damage can be found in Appendix A - Items Properties Index on pg. 91. docs.title: Cooking
docs.text: Perhaps, instead of dealing damage, you'd like to make your friends a delicious meal or two. Eating carefully prepared meals can provide some minor benefits. A GM may decide cooking provides temporary Ability buffs lasting 1d6 hours per tier of meal. Or, it may provide some minor healing, 1d6 per tier plus the Material Cost of the type of meal consumed. This is one of the few crafting types that does not take nearly as long as others. Using the Jury-Rig Expertise with this could cause unexpected side effects, randomly rolled from the Complications and Bonuses Chart, its effects last 2d6 hours after consumption.  Under normal circumstances, gathering food should not cause any undue pressure. The only real limit in this circumstance would be the character's ability, and how good they think they can cook. Should your characters find themselves in a difficult situation, however, such as lost in the woods, deep behind enemy lines, or what have you, the GM could readily decide that tracking your food stores is essential in said situation. docs.title: Electronics
docs.text: You enjoy building electronic equipment from scratch, whether audio and video equipment, timers, radios, or listening devices. When building an electronic device, be sure to run the description of the kind of device you wish to construct. Depending on your explanation, the GM then decides on a tier for that particular piece of equipment in terms of how it might compare to similar items of its type. docs.title: Explosives
docs.text: Building an explosive device from scratch is dangerous work. If the crafting check fails by 4 or less, the materials are simply wasted and permanently inert. A failure by 5 or more, however, and the explosive compound will prematurely detonate, dealing half of its intended damage to the builder and anyone else within a 10 yard radius. If the check succeeds, the final product is a stable, solid material, about the size of a brick, weighing 1 pound per tier. docs.text:
Similar to the Electronics entry, when it comes to building a device or gadget that would fall under Mechanical crafting (i.e.: firearms, vehicle parts), players describe their device to the GM, who determines by the description what tier the item would fall under. This decision could potentially be affected by the device's proximity to current technology. Scraps

Like Food, Scraps is an ambiguous term, representing virtually any form of building material. This has been done to streamline the Item Crafting processing, but is only a suggestion. GMs and Players should discuss whether or not more specificity is necessary.

docs.title: Pharmaceutical
docs.text: With the right training, you can compound medicinal drugs to aid in speeding up someone's natural healing processes,  defend themselves against treatable illnesses and poisons, or grant temporary boosts. This is by no means an all-inclusive list. If you can think of a way to use this that isn't listed, discuss your ideas with your GM to determine the item you're wanting to make and its efficacy.
