docs.title: Poisonous Substances
docs.text:
Some days, the very thing you need can be as simple as picking a flower, or as complex as mixing chemicals together into caustic and dangerous brews. The items listed in this section are a little bit different from the rest, in that some require specific Expertise to be used, handling methods, and application methods. Some poisons, as indicated below, have multiple effects. The first effect happens immediately on application of the poison. And, if left uncured for 5 minutes (20 rounds), the second effect happens. If left uncured for another minute (4 rounds), the effects become permanent. Poisonous Substances Required Toolkit: Portable Laboratory, Bartender's Kit Poison/Application Material Cost Effect Arsenic / Ingested Chemicals (5) 1 Damage to Might / 2 Damage to Constitution Atropine / Injury Chemicals (5) 1 Damage to Reflexes / 1 Damage to Might Belladonna / Injury Cooking (CR 20-25) 1 Damage to Might / 2 Damage to Might Chloroform / Inhaled Chemicals (5) Incapacitated Condition for 1d6 hours / Brain-Damage Condition Curare / Injury Cooking (CR 20-25) 2 Damage to Reflexes / 2 Damage to Willpower Cyanide / Injury or Ingested Chemicals (5) 1 Damage to Constitution / 2 Damage to Constitution Tear Gas / Inhaled Chemicals (5) Suffocation Condition for 1d6 Minutes / Blinded Condition for 1d6 Hours VX Nerve Gas / Inhaled Chemicals (5) 2 Damage to Constitution / 2 Damage to Constitution GM Note

In some circumstances, poisons could quickly become problematic if overused. Many of the actual chemicals used to craft these substances are illegal. GMs and Players are highly encouraged to discuss limits on acquiring or crafting Poisonous Substances, and/or establish more specificity in what chemicals are required.

docs.title: How This Works
docs.text:
Not all items are created with equal toughness in mind. This sub-chapter presents rules for attacking and damaging items, vehicles, and structures. The purpose for this mechanic is to give another layer of tactical edge to the system as a whole. Items can be targeted by enemy attacks. Buildings and vehicles can be hit by rockets, or demolished with planted explosives. And, most unfortunately, sometimes bullets don't care about what taking cover might mean to you.

In terms of direct attacks, items and equipment must be targeted via Called Shot for sundering. This Called Shot is made at a Minor Disadvantage. For additional Called Shot rules, see pg. 88. Splash, Blast, or otherwise area affecting attacks usually deal damage to everything within a certain space.

Any time an item is hit, or if an attack roll would hit a target behind full cover, or if the target is missed by 2 or less, the item or cover's AP soaks up a portion of the damage. Subtract the AP from the attack's damage, representing the absorption of some of the impact. If the damage exceeds the cover or item's AP, any remaining damage goes through to what's behind it.

Every time the AP of the item or cover in question is breached, the item's AP is reduced by an amount equal to the attacking character's Chance Die. Standard Rounds always go against whatever the current AP is. Armor-Piercing Rounds ignore a portion or bypass AP altogether. Explosives or Acid generally degrade larger areas of items or cover at any given time, potentially destroying it completely.

Some objects are harder than others to destroy. You can't bring a building down with a rifle, after all. When it comes to structures and hardened materials, even if its AP becomes 0, then without actual demolitions, it cannot be destroyed, and functions at the Quarter Damage Threshold until destroyed with the proper ordnance.

Some items have thresholds of damaged AP, at which their utility and effectiveness are decreased to the point of malfunctioning or losing functionality altogether.

AP Threshold Effect Full The item works in its optimal condition, with little to no issues. Half The item begins malfunctioning, imposing Minor Disadvantage on all rolls associated with the item. Quarter Severe malfunctions occur, imposing Major Disadvantage on all rolls associated with the item. The item has a chance to stop working temporarily. If a player rolls doubles while using this item, it ceases to function for a number of rounds equal to the Chance Die. 0 AP The item is destroyed, too damaged to protect, or is otherwise completely useless.

docs.title: Soft Cover
docs.text:
Examples: Curtains, cloth, thin wooden panels

AP Value: 1 - 2

Description: Minimal protection, easily penetrated by most weapons. Soft cover is generally destroyed after one or two breaches.

docs.title: Light Cover
docs.text:
Examples: Thick wooden furniture, drywall, car door, dumpster

AP Value: 3 - 5

Description: Provides some protection, but can be penetrated by most firearms.

docs.title: Medium Cover
docs.text:
Examples: Concrete wall, HVAC Unit

AP Value: 6 - 8

Description: Offers substantial protection against small arms fire.

docs.title: Heavy Cover
docs.text:
Examples: Steel barriers, vehicle engine block, armored vehicle parts

AP Value: 9 - 12

Description: Resistant to most small arms and provides excellent protection.

docs.title: Reinforced Cover
docs.text:
Example: Bunkers, thick steel, building supports

AP Value: 13 +

Description: Nearly impervious to all but the most powerful weapons or explosives. Typically, can only be damaged by Heavy Arms or Explosives.

docs.title: Traits
docs.text:
What a Trait Represents

A Trait represents focused training or experience, not raw talent. As such:

  • Traits do not grant new actions unless explicitly stated.
  • Traits provide a situational bonus to Action Rolls where applicable.
  • Traits may be referenced by other rules (Expertise, Chance Actions, Equipment), but are not locked to them (if another Trait could apply, speak to your GM).
  • Traits never stack with other Traits on the same Action Roll.

A Trait is a focused area of proficiency within its related ability. A Trait placed in a Primary Ability grants a +3 bonus when making an Action Roll related to it. This bonus is only a +2 for Secondary Ability Traits. The GM will usually let you know what Trait applies to each test, but if it isn't clear don't be afraid to ask if a Trait applies.

Starting at Level 11, you may select a Trait you already have, to increase your bonus with it by 1. This increases Primary Ability Traits to a +4, and Secondary Ability Traits to a +3.

Variable Traits are those that don't fit cleanly into any single category. When you take a Trait in the Variable list, you can apply it to one of your Ability Scores. You must be able to justify your use of a Trait should your choice be questionable. Be sure to discuss these with your GM to determine.

Creating Unique Traits

If a GM or Player can justify the creation of a Trait, feel free to do so. GM's, especially, should do this for high-profile NPCs and elite enemies.

The primary caveat, of course, is to be sure the scope of your Trait is important enough to give you an edge, but limited in scope to keep the game fair.

Info

Note that Traits tagged with (Weapon Group) do not give you Weapon Group training. Weapon Group Training, however, is a prerequisite for taking one of these Traits. See #character-creation, or #equipment.

docs.title: Variable Traits
docs.text: Athletics Executing physical maneuvers, including climbing, running, and grappling. Craftsmanship Producing high-quality items. When combined with a Crafting Expertise, grants an additional Minor Advantage (+2) on the Test. Intimidation Influencing others through threats, posture, or sheer presence. Light Melee (Weapon Group) Fighting with weapons from the Light Melee group, often usable with either Might or Reflexes. Multitasking Managing multiple actions simultaneously. Once per encounter, gain an additional Minor Action. Profession Performing specialized career or trade-oriented tasks.

docs.title: Might Traits
docs.text: Archaic Melee (Weapon Group) Fighting with melee weapons from pre-modern eras. Climbing Scaling vertical surfaces and overcoming obstacles. Heavy Melee (Weapon Group) Fighting with large, high-impact melee weapons. Improvised Melee (Weapon Group) Fighting with handheld objects not designed as weapons. Jumping Covering distance through leaps and vaults. Martial Arts (Weapon Group) Fighting Unarmed, using trained techniques and specialized stances. Smithing Forging and shaping metal into weapons, armor, and tools. Strength Performing feats of raw power, such as lifting, carrying, or breaking objects.

docs.title: Reflexes Traits
docs.text: Acrobatics Performing gymnastic maneuvers, balancing, and tumbling. Archaic Ranged (Weapon Group) Fighting with ranged weapons from pre-modern eras. Calligraphy Writing with precision and artistic flair. CQB Using ranged weapons effectively in melee combat. Crafting Making items with precise manual skills like woodworking, sculpting, glassblowing, etc. Driving Directing and guiding motorized ground or naval vehicles. Initiative Acting quickly in tense situations. Lock Picking Opening locks without using keys. Piloting Directing and guiding an air vehicle. Riding Directing a mount or non-motorized wheeled vehicle. Sleight of Hand Using tricks of movement to manipulate objects in unsuspected ways. Stealth Sneaking about quietly and out of sight. Thrown Weapons (Weapon Group) Fighting with weapons that are most effective when thrown.

docs.title: Constitution Traits
docs.text: Breath Control Keeping your breathing even and steady in situations where breathing may be difficult. Drinking Consuming larger quantities of alcohol and avoiding the after-effects. Immunity Avoiding the effects of various substances like infections and diseases. Running Moving quickly in both short sprints and long-distance hauls. Stamina Enduring fatigue and privation. Swimming Moving through liquid and staying afloat where needed.

docs.title: Wits Traits
docs.text: Brewing Making ales, wines, and other alcohol-based concoctions. Computer Use Operating a personal computer, or other device. Cryptography Creating and deciphering codes and ciphers. Cultural Lore Knowing the traditions and beliefs of various cultures. Design Planning the creation of an item. Electrical Knowing the practicalities of electrical circuits. Engineering Knowing the practicalities of mechanical construction and building. Explosives (Weapon Group) Handling, constructing, dismantling, and operating weapons from the Explosives group. Evaluation Determining the value of goods and items. Healing Aiding the wounded and sick. Heavy-Arms (Weapon Group) Fighting with specialized heavy projectile weapons. Historical Lore Knowing important events and personalities from the past. Jury-Rigging Knowing how to build temporary items to perform specific tasks. Military Lore Knowing music theory, tradition, and songs. Natural Lore Knowing flora and fauna, and their potential uses. Navigation Planning and following a route from one place to another. Poison Lore Knowing about poisonous substances, their uses, and their preparation. Research Making a systematic investigation, usually using records, archives, and books. Religious Lore Knowing religious traditions and practices. Writing Expressing yourself with the written word.

docs.title: Willpower Traits
docs.text: Animal Handling Interacting with and caring for animals. Bargaining Negotiating with others and making deals. Courage Maintaining personal confidence and refusing to succumb to fear effects. Deception Lying to and tricking others using fast talk and subterfuge. Etiquette Knowing the social niceties of various cultures. Faith Deriving inner strength through spiritual or moral belief. Leadership Guiding, directing, and inspiring others. Morale Maintaining good spirits and confidence in yourself or your group. Performance Entertaining an audience with an artistic expertise. Persuasion Convincing others to agree with you. Self-Discipline Focusing your mental energy or controlling your impulses and emotions.

docs.title: Perception Traits
docs.text:
Empathy Discerning the feelings, emotions, and intentions of others. Hearing Using your auditory sense to notice and identify things. Improvised Range (Weapon Group) Fighting with projectile-launching objects not normally classified as weapons. Long-Arms (Weapon Group) Fighting with long-barelled firearms. Searching Finding things that are hidden or obscured. Seeing Using your visual sense to notice and identify things. Small-Arms (Weapon Group) Fighting with small hand-held firearms. Smelling Using your olfactory sense to notice and identify things. Tracking Following footprints and other signs of passage through an area.

Info

The Bargaining, Leadership, and Profession Traits each multiply RP gained from monthly wages, leveling, and missions; by 2, 3, or 4, depending on Trait level and whether the trait is Primary or Secondary. The benefits from these Traits do not stack.

docs.title: Working in Multiple Disciplines
docs.text:
Cross-Training at a Glance

  • Gain the benefits of a second Background
  • Recieve:
    • 1 Weapon Group (if available)
    • 1 Background Benefit
    • +10 RP Income
  • Can be taken twice (with expanded options at Level 11+)

Some people career hop. Some go into dual-degree programs. There isn't a one-size-fits-all categorization of background experiences. Since the backgrounds in TNC function effectively like classes in other systems, and being trained for various applications of skill is a real-life experience, these Traits operate outside of the normal Trait rules. Like Variable Traits, these can potentially fit under multiple Abilities, and can therefore be listed under any of them.

Instead of the normal bonus gained by taking a Trait, these options allow you to pick up an additional Background, and grant you the following from the chosen secondary Background: 1 Weapon Group Training (if available), 1 chosen Background Benefit, and their monthly RP earnings increases by +10 for leveling and monthly income purchases. If taken a second time at or beyond level 11, the PC can acquire 1 additional Background training, using the above rules, which stack with the first. Alternatively, they can gain 1 additional Background benefit from their first cross-training, and their RP earnings increase by another (for a maximum of +20). This does not affect Mission income.

docs.title: Cross-Training Traits
docs.text: Athlete Training Blue Collar Training Law Enforcement Training Medicine Training Military Training Scholar Training White Collar Training

docs.title: Expertises
docs.text:
Expertise is what separates the commanders from their rank-and-file counterparts. As characters grow in their abilities, it's likely they'll specialize in mastering their disciplines. Prerequisite training required to begin training in an expertise is indicated in its block.

Each Expertise comes in four tiers, which must be taken in order: Basic, which covers the bare minimum of training required to use an Expertise's abilities. Initiate, which indicates a moderate level of training. Advanced, which is the point at which mastery begins to truly show. And, finally, Expert, which indicates true, full mastery in a given Expertise.

The Expertises listed are separated into categories, indicating how they would most likely be used.

docs.title: Agile Defense Styles
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Defense, Melee, Reaction, Mobility

Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts, No Equipped Armor

Defense is not optional for you. Your art is your bond. Your spirit is your guide. You seek safety in the heart of danger.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Reactive defense and counter-pressure Playstyle: Stay mobile, bait attacks, punish mistakes

Benefits Basic: +2 Defense, Speed Boost Initiate: Improved Defense Bonuses, Improvised Cover Advanced: Swift-Shift (1 CP), Brutal Counter (2 CP) Expert: Passive Defense +5, Unique Feature - Only The Strong

You gain +2 Defense.

Once per round, when you are targeted by an attack, your Speed increases by 1 + ½ level (round down) yards until the start of your next turn.

Using the Defend Minor Action while in melee combat grants you a bonus to your Defense equal to half of the Defense Bonus of one chosen opponent within your Melee Range until the start of your next turn.

A target chosen this way becomes Improvised Cover against Ranged Attack Rolls and Area Effect attacks.

Additionally, when you use the Defend Action, you gain a Major Advantage on your next attack roll against a target within your Melee Range. If this benefit is not used before the end of your next turn, it is lost.

  • Swift-Shift (1 CP): Use Defend as an Instinct Action.
  • Brutal Counter (2 CP): If an enemy misses their attack within your Melee Range due to your use of the Defend Action, you may add half of your Reflexes Ability and the target's last used Attack Bonus to your next Melee Damage Roll. If your attack already uses Reflexes, use your Willpower Ability for the bonus instead.
  • Defense bonus increases to +5.
  • Only The Strong: If an enemy misses you with an attack within your Melee Range due to your use of the Defend Action, that enemy takes your Unarmed or Melee Attack Damage as part of their action.

docs.title: Archery Style
docs.text:
Type: Style Tags: Offense, Ranged Prerequisites: Trained with Archaic Ranged Weapons; Archaic Ranged Weapon Equipped

Your arm is steady, and your aim is true. Although your weapon has been overshadowed by conventional fighting methods, that doesn't mean it can be safely overlooked.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Active offense

Benefits Basic: Ignore 4 AP; Increased Range. Initiate: +2 Aim; Faster Reload. Advanced: Faster Attack Expert: Ignore Cover

Your Ranged Attacks using an Archaic Ranged Weapon gain the Armor-Piercing Property, ignoring up to 4 points of a target's AP.

Additionally, increase all Weapon Range Increments with these weapons by 5 + 1/2 level (round down) yards.

  • Focused Shot: When you use the Aim Minor Action, gain an additional +2 bonus on all Ranged Attack Rolls until the end of your turn.
  • Rapid Reload: You can reload a bowgun as a Minor Action. All other Archaic Ranged Weapons can be reloaded as a Free Action.

  • Rain of Arrows: While using an Archaic Ranged Weapon, you can treat your Ranged Attack Roll with this kind of weapon as a Minor Action.

  • Additionally, you can perform the Lightning Attack for 2 CP instead of the usual 3.

  • Masterful Aim: While using an Archaic Ranged Weapon, you can ignore up to 3/4 cover.

docs.title: Bladesman Style
docs.text:
Type: Style Tags: Offense, Melee Prerequisites: Trained with Archaic Melee Weapons; Melee Weapon Equipped

You have studied ancient weapons and fighting styles, finding a practical use for them in the modern era. A pillar of dedication, you stand strong in the face of danger.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Active offense

Benefits Basic: +2 Defense Initiate: Parry (2 CP) Advanced: Deep Wounds (1+ CP) Expert: Cull The Weak (9 CP)

As long as you wield a bladed melee weapon with a Minimum Might Requirement of 3 or less, gain a +2 bonus to your Defense. Heavier weapons only allow a +1 bonus to your Defense.

  • Perfect Parry: When you are targeted by a Melee Attack, you can roll an opposing Melee Attack Roll with a +1 bonus to the roll, instead of relying on Defense. If you succeed, you manage to push the enemy's attack away. If you roll doubles, gain a Minor Advantage (+2) on your next Melee Attack Roll. If you roll triples, you can make one immediate Melee Attack as a Non-Action.
  • Deep Wounds (1+ CP): Instead of dealing direct damage, you can impose a number of points of the Bleeding Condition equal to the amount of CP spent into this Chance Action.
  • Cull The Weak (9 CP): While your target is suffering the Bleeding Condition, you can use this unique Chance Action. Roll a Melee Attack as a Major Action, regardless of any effects that increase your attack speed. On a successful hit, you gain a cumulative +1 bonus to your Melee Attack Damage per point of Bleeding Condition currently applied to your target.

docs.title: Boxing Style
docs.text:
Type: Style Tags: Offense, Melee Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts; No Weapon Equipped

You can float, and you can sting. Any proper fighter should be good at using their fists when possible, as pugilism is the way of the strong.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Active offense

Benefits Basic: +2 Damage Initiate: +Per Defense Advanced: Hard Hook (1 CP) Expert: Rope-A-Dope (5 CP)

When you make an Unarmed Attack, you can spend your Minor Action to increase your Unarmed Damage by +2.

On any round where you have not moved two yards or more, you gain half your Perception as a Bonus to your Defense.

  • Hard Hook (2+ CP): On any round where you have moved at least two yards or more, you can ignore up to 1 AP per 2 CP spent, provided by Light or Medium Armor with your Unarmed Attack.
  • Rope-A-Dope (4 CP): After you have used the Hard Hook Chance Action, until the start of your next turn, any Melee Attacks you are targeted and missed by cause your Attacker to gain Winded (T1+) Condition, the Tier of Winded increasing for every missed Melee Attack.

docs.title: CQB Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Offense, Melee, Ranged

Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts; Ranged Weapon Equipped

Close quarters excite you. You've made being too close to an enemy into an artform.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Balanced Offense/Defense

Benefits Basic: Activate + Aim on Initiative, Hard to Disarm Initiate: Riposte 2 CP, Ranged Counterattack Advanced: Double-Tap (2 CP) Expert: Moving Target (4 CP)

You can use the Activate and Aim Minor Actions as part of your Initiative Roll. Additionally, any attempt to Disarm you is made at a Major Disadvantage (-5).

As long as you are within CQB range of an opponent, you can use the Riposte Chance Action as an Instinct Action for 2 CP instead of the usual 4. Additionally, you can use a Ranged Attack for the Riposte counterattack instead of a Melee Attack. This allows you to use Riposte against Ranged Attacks that miss you.

  • Double-Tap (2 CP): You can make a single Ranged Attack Roll count as making 2 Ranged Attacks using the same Major Action, dealing damage with both as normal. These attacks must be aimed at the same target, and you must have at least two pieces of ammunition loaded into your weapon.
  • Moving Target (4 CP): When you are targeted by a Ranged Attack while in CQB range with another enemy, you can use your Instinct Action to impose a Major Disadvantage (-5) on the attack roll. If the attack misses, you can immediately move up to 2 yards without provoking attacks of opportunity. If the attack misses by 5 or less, it hits the enemy you were in CQB range with instead of you.

docs.title: Crusher Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Offense, Melee

Prerequisites: Might 2 or Higher, Trained in Strength, Melee Weapon Equipped

Why wait for the tables to turn, when you can just hit them with a chair?

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Aggressive Offense

Benefits Basic: +4 Damage Initiate: Double Footwork Distance Advanced: Chair Breaker (1+ CP), Armor Breaker (3+ CP) Expert: Ability Damage

Whenever you roll doubles on an Attack Roll with a Bludgeoning Melee Weapon, you deal an additional +4 Damage.

For every CP spent into the Footwork Chance Action, double the distance you push your target. Any target moved more than 3 yards this way must make a Reflexes Saving Throw (CR 9 + half yards moved) or fall prone.

  • Chair Breaker (1+ CP): You may spend up to 6 CP. You deal extra damage equal to the amount of CP spent, plus your weapon's effective AP. Doing so damages your Melee Weapon. Every CP spent causes the weapon to lose one AP Threshold until it's Completely Destroyed.
  • Armor Breaker (3+ CP): Instead of dealing direct damage, you can attempt to destroy a target's armor. A successful hit forces the target to make a Reflexes Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Might). On a failure, the target's armor loses one AP Threshold per 3 CP spent, until Completely Destroyed.

Whenever you roll doubles on an Attack Roll with a Bludgeoning Melee Weapon, you deal your Might in Damage to one of your target's Abilities. This damage persists for a number of rounds equal to your Chance Die. If you roll triples, the damage persists a number of days equal to your Chance Die.

docs.title: Iron Giant Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Defense

Prerequisites: Hardened Skin Mutation; or Light, Medium, or Heavy Armor equipped.

Your armor is almost a second skin to you. You've trained extensively in its effective uses, and as such are not as hindered as others.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Defense

Benefits Basic: 1/2 AP to Speed Initiate: Stand Firm as Instinct Action, Armor is 1/2 Cover. Advanced: Bear The Brunt (3 CP) Expert: Determined Protector

You take only half of your Armor's penalty to your Speed. Additionally, you ignore your Armor's penalty to other Reflexes-based Action Rolls.

You can use Stand Firm as an Instinct Action, instead of as a Minor Action. Additionally, your Armor counts as 1/2 Cover for you.

  • Bear The Brunt (3 CP): You can ignore Armor Piercing from all sources, including from Chance Actions, until the start of your next turn.
  • Determined Protector: If an Attack targets any character other than you, you can use your Instinct Action to interpose yourself, automatically taking the hit. Targets protected in this way benefit from you as 3/4ths Cover. The target of this ability must be within 6 yards to benefit from this action.

docs.title: Knife Fighting Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Offense, Melee

Prerequisites: Reflexes 3 or Higher, Trained in Archaic Melee or Light Melee; One-Handed Light Melee Weapon Equipped

You laugh in the face of the idiot that brought a gun to a knife fight.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Offense

Benefits Basic: +2 Damage Initiate: Reverse Grip Advanced: Stick-and-Move (1+ CP) Expert: Between The Plates (6 CP)

You deal an additional +2 bonus to Damage when Attacking with a one-handed Light Melee Weapon that deals Slashing Damage.

Reverse Grip: You can use a Minor Action to flip the Light Melee Weapon over in your hand to a reverse grip, improving your control at the cost of distance and damage. While this is active, your Melee Attack Range becomes 1 yard, and you gain a +5 bonus to Attack Rolls. Doing so imposes a -5 penalty to your Damage.

  • (Stick-and-Move 1+ CP): On a successful hit, you impose a Minor Disadvantage (-2) on your target's next attack. If used more than once, this becomes a Major Disadvantage (-5). An attack that misses you because of this ability allows you to launch a single counterattack as an Instinct Action. Instead of dealing damage, this counterattack imposes an amount of Bleeding Tiers equal to the amount of Chance Points spent into it.
  • (6 CP): When you perform a Called Shot, your attack forces the target to roll their Saving Throw against suffering a Condition, regardless of equipment or armor that would normally protect against the effects of Called Shots.

docs.title: Lone Wolf Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Defense

Prerequisites: Perception 3 or Higher, Reflexes 3 or Higher, Trained in Martial Arts

Your opponents' final thoughts are generally about how foolish it was to surround you.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Defense

Benefits Basic: Can't be outnumbered. Initiate: Bonus Defense, Dual Strike (2 CP) Advanced: Dance of the Reaper (1+ CP) Expert: Broken Pack

As long as you remain outnumbered, you ignore Advantage gained on attacks targeting you due to number of enemies.

As long as you remain outnumbered, your Defense gains a bonus equal to the number of targets whose Advantage has been ignored. Additionally, you can use the Dual Strike Chance Action for 2 CP instead of 4.

  • Dance of the Reaper(1+ CP): Your movement speed increases by 5 yards per CP spent. Make a series of Melee or Ranged attacks against a number of targets equal to CP spent, as long as you can reasonably reach them with your movement. This is made as a single Attack Action Roll, compared to each target's Defense, dealing Chance Damage on each, unless you roll triples, in which case you deal Critical Damage.
  • Broken Pack: As long as you remain outnumbered, you gain Major Advantage on a number of your Attacks equal to the number of targets whose Advantage was ignored.

docs.title: Master Key Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Offense

Prerequisites: Trained in Long-Arms

Doors are not a barrier to you, so long as you have ammunition in your shotgun. Enemies don't fare much better.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Battlefield Control

Benefits Basic: Shotguns gain Destructive property Initiate: All Shotguns Semi-Auto Advanced: Door-Kicker, Short-Stocking Expert: Point-Blank Master (9 CP), Rapid Breach (3 CP)

A shotgun you wield gains the Destructive property, ignoring up to 10 AP when used against objects not being worn or carried.

You can rack your shotgun almost as fast as you can fire it. You may treat a shotgun with the Single Shot property as though it has the Semi-Auto property, allowing it to fire multiple shots per turn.

  • Door-Kicker (1 CP): You've mastered the art of transitioning from breaching to combat. After firing a ranged weapon, you can move up to half your speed in yards. This allows you to maintain momentum when breaching doors, moving through cover, or maintaining tactical superiority.
  • Short-Stocking: When engaging a target within Short Range or less with a Long-Arms weapon, you gain a bonus to your Ranged Attack Damage equal to your Reflexes.
  • Rapid Breach (2 CP): You can fire your shotgun at an object that is not being worn or carried (such as a door or barrier), as a Free Action. This allows you to quickly move through doors or cover and immediately take your next combat action without losing time.

docs.title: MMA Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Adaptive

Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts

You have trained in a variety of Martial Arts Styles, adapting what you like and discarding the rest. No opponent knows quite what to expect from you next.

Note: Benefits from this style do not stack with their original style's benefits (for example, you can only use Basic Striking Style's benefits using MMA Style or Striking Style, but not both at the same time).

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role: Adaptable, Offense, Defense

Benefits Basic: Any Basic Style, switch 5 times per Rest. Initiate: Any Initiate Style, switch 2 times per Rest. Advanced: Any Advanced Style, switch 1 time per Rest. Expert: Any Expert Style, Fixed.

Pick one Basic Tier Ability of any Expertise with the Style Tag. 5 times per Rest, as a Free Action, you can swap this ability for another Basic Tier Ability. You can only gain the benefits from one Basic Tier Ability in this way at a time.

Pick one Initiate Tier Ability of any Expertise with the Style tag. 2 times per Rest, as a Minor Action, you can swap this ability for another Initiate Tier Ability. You can only gain the benefits from one Initiate Tier Ability in this way at a time.

  • Pick one Advanced Tier Ability of any Expertise with the Style tag. 1 time per rest, as a Major Action, you can swap this ability for another Advanced Tier Ability. You can only gain the benefits from one Advanced Tier Ability in this way at a time.
  • Pick one Expert Tier Ability of any Expertise with the Style Label. This ability can only be swapped during an extended Rest (i.e.: as a downtime activity). You can only gain the benefits from one Expert Tier Ability in this way at a time.

docs.title: Rifleman Style
docs.text:
Type: Style

Tags: Ranged, Offense

Prerequisites: Trained in Long-Arms

There are many weapons like it, but this one is yours. When all else forsakes you, your rifle is your unconditional friend.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role:

Benefits Basic: +2 Attack with Long-Arms Initiate: Aim as a Free Action Advanced: Suppressive Fire (4 CP) Expert: Rapid Suppression (2 CP)

Your familiarity with long barelled weapons grants you a +2 bonus to Attack Rolls with Long-Arms.

Once per Round, you can use Aim as a Free Action.

  • Suppressive Fire (4 CP): You make a Ranged Attack targeting a 10 yard cone. Instead of rolling, all targets in that cone must make a Reflexes Saving Throw (CR 9 + your Perception). On a failed save, they take your weapon's damage, altered as normal by doubles or triples. On a successful save, they take no Damage. Targets benefitting from 3/4ths cover automatically succeed on this save.
  • Rapid Suppression (2 CP): As an Expert Rifleman, your precision and timing are unmatched, allowing you to perform the unique Suppressive Fire Chance Action for 2 CP instead of the usual 4. THis mastery of battlefield control lets you dictate the flow of combat. Cover no longer allows automatic success on the Saving Throw.

docs.title: Small-Arms Expert Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites: Trained in Small-Arms

Your skill with small firearms is something to behold.

OverviewBasic

Role:

Benefits Basic: Draw and Holster as a Non-Action. Reload as an Instinct Action. Initiate: Aim gives +2 bonus. Advanced: Deadeye Shot (2 CP) Expert: Lethal Focus

You can draw and holster a Small-Arms weapon as a Non-Action. Additionally, you can reload a Small-Arms Weapon as an Instinct Action.

Initiate

When you use Aim while wielding a Small-Arms Weapon, you gain a +2 bonus to your Attack Roll instead of the usual +1.

AdvancedExpert
  • Deadeye Shot (2 CP): Once per Round, you can reroll a single failed Attack Roll with a Small-Arms weapon, and may use either result.
  • Lethal Focus: While using a Small-Arms Weapon, if you roll Doubles, your Damage ignores up to 4 AP provided by a target's armor or cover. If you roll triples, your Damage ignores up to 6 AP provided by a target's armor or cover.

docs.title: Stick Fighting Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts, Trained in Archaic Melee, Light Melee, or Heavy Melee; Melee Weapon Equipped

You have focused your training into lengthy or archaic weapons such as staves or spears.

OverviewBasic

Role:

Benefits Basic: Melee Range becomes 4 yards Initiate: Stand Firm as Instinct Action with +2 bonus Advanced: Heart Strike (1 CP), Sweeping Strike Expert: Whirling Staff (4 CP)

Using the length of your weapon to your advantage, you treat enemies up to 4 yards away as if they were adjacent to you, so you can make Melee Attacks against them (they can't do the same unless they're also using this Expertise). If your way to that target is blocked by cover or another character, you can still attack that target using your Melee Weapon, with a penalty to the Attack Roll equal to the AP of the cover used.

Initiate

You can use the Stand Firm Chance Action as an Instinct Action, following its other rules as normal, but with a +2 bonus to your resistance against Footwork or Knock Prone. Additionally, if your weapon has the Double Property, you can now attack twice with the same Major Action.

AdvancedExpert
  • Heart Strike (1 CP): You can perform the Pierce Armor Chance Action for 1 CP instead of the usual 2.
  • Sweeping Strike: You can perform the Knock Prone Chance Action as a Free Action.
  • Whirling Staff (4 CP): You move your weapon in a devastating spiral, striking down enemies with brutal efficiency. As a Major Action, you can make a single Melee Attack against all enemies considered adjacent to you. Each enemy hit must make a Constitution Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Level) or take your weapon's Chance Damage and be Dazed until the end of your next turn.

docs.title: Striking Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites:

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role:

Benefits Basic: 2 Attacks per Round Initiate: 3 Attacks per Round, Trap and Box (1 CP) Advanced: Precision Strike (2 CP) Expert: 1 Instinct Action Attack per Round, or 4 Attacks per Round

Make an Unarmed Attack as a Minor Action, allowing you up to 2 Attacks per Round.

Make an Unarmed Attack as a Free Action, allowing you up to 3 Attacks per Round.

  • Trap and Box (1 CP): When attacked, instead of relying on your regular Defense, you can roll an opposed Unarmed Attack. On a success, you stop the target's attack and initiate a Grapple, and your next Unarmed Attack gains +2 Damage. On a failure, the target's Attack deals an additional +2 damage.
  • Precision Strike (2 CP): When using a Chance Action that increases damage in combination with your Unarmed Attack, add an additional +4 to that Damage.

Make an Unarmed Attack as an Instinct Action, allowing up to 4 Attacks per Round. This would alternatively allow 1 Attack as an Instinct Action on another character's turn, and up to 3 regular Attacks. This does not grant additional Instinct Actions.

  • Ultra Combination: While using the Lightning Attack Chance Action, when you roll Doubles, gain additional, cumulative Chance Points equal to your Might or Reflexes (whichever is higher). These points must be used for additional Lightning Attacks immediately, or be lost. You can gain these points a number of times equal to your Might or Reflexes (whichever is higher) in additional Chance Actions per Round through this ability.

docs.title: Submission Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites: Trained in Martial Arts, Trained in Strength

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role:

Benefits Basic: Ignore Defense penalties from Grapples. Initiate: Grapple as a Minor Action. Advanced: Ground-and-Pound (3 CP) Expert: Kraken Grapple (2 CP)

You can ignore the penalties to Defense from Grappling.

You can use Grapple as a Minor Action.

  • Ground-And-Pound (3 CP): A target grappled by you must make a Might Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Might Ability). On a failure, both you and the target become Prone. While Prone, you can use your Free Action to immediately deal Grapple Strike Damage twice.
  • Sleeper Hold (6+ CP): You maneuver to your target's throat and clench. The target makes a Constitution Saving Throw (CR 9 + your Might) to resist, with the CR increasing every round the Grapple is held. On a failed roll, target gains 1 Tier of the Fatigue Condition. For every additional 6 CP spent, increase the CR increase and the Tier count of the Condition by +1 (maximum +3 per round, with a maximum Tier of 5, and a maximum CR of 20).
  • Kraken Grapple (2 CP): Your Grapple Strikes gain a +2 bonus to Damage. Additionally, your Grapple Strike Unarmed Attacks gain the Armor-Piercing Property, ignoring up to 4 of a target's AP.
  • Full Lock (12 CP): Spend all of your Actions for the turn to immediately place your target at Tier 5 Entangled. Target makes a Might or Reflexes Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Might) to resist.

docs.title: Thai Boxer Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites:

A dangerous, brutal style for those that don't mind being close to their opponents. Your elbows and knees do the job right.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role:

Benefits Basic: Push a target away. Initiate: Unarmed Attack Range -1 yard, Damage +3. Advanced: Whip Kick (2 CP) Expert: Impact Finish (9 CP)

When you roll Doubles on an Unarmed Attack Roll, you can choose to push your targetup to 1+ your Chance Die in yards, in a straight line away from you. A target that is moved more than 3 yards in this way must make a Reflexes Saving Throw (CR 8 + yards moved) or fall Prone.

Spend a Minor Action. For the next minute (4 rounds) of combat, your Unarmed Attack Range is decreased to 1 yard, but your Unarmed Attack Damage is increased by +3. Additionally, when you choose to Push on Doubles, as long as you have movement and attacks left available, you can follow the target and attack again.

  • Whip Kick (2 CP): Choose one of three points on your target; Low, Middle, or High. Instead of dealing Damage on a sucessful hit, your target must make a Constitution Saving Throw (CR 10 + your Might Ability). Failure causes one Tier of one of these Conditions (Half their Chance Die, rounded down):

  • Low: Shaken, Slowed, or Prone

  • Middle: Nauseated, Weakened, or Winded.

  • High: Confused, Dazed, or Enraged

  • Full Onslaught (6 CP): When a target is suffering any Condition caused by your Whip Kick Chance Action, you can deliver a devastating follow-up attack. Make an additional Unarmed Attack Roll as an Instinct Action. If the attack hits, the target takes an additional +1 Damage per Condition inflicted by Whip Kick (max. +3). The target must then make a Saving Throw (CR 11 + your Chance Die), determined by your last successful Whip Kick Height:

  • Low: Might Save

  • Middle: Constitution Save

  • High: Willpower Save

On a failed Save, the target becomes Helpless for a number of rounds equal to your Chance Die.

docs.title: Wrestling Style
docs.text:
Type:

Tags:

Prerequisites: Trained in Strength

Throws and takedowns are child's play for you. Anyone getting close enough to grab is going to have a bad day if you have anything to say about it.

OverviewBasicInitiateAdvancedExpert

Role:

Benefits Basic: +2 bonus to Grapples Initiate: Free Action Grapple after Unarmed Hit Advanced: Slip Grab (2+ CP), Suplex (1+ CP) Expert: Iron Grip (4 CP), Throw Combination (6+ CP), Hurricane Throw (6 CP)

Gain a +2 bonus to all Action Rolls related to Grapples.

When you successfully hit a target with an Unarmed Attack, you can make an immediate Grapple attempt as a Free Action.

  • Slip Grab (2+ CP): If you are missed by a Melee or Unarmed Attack, you can perform a special Grapple and Throw maneuver as an Instinct Action, dealing your normal Grapple Strike Damage. Your target lands Prone a number of yards away equal to your Chance Die. You can keep hold of the Grapple and refuse the throw distance by spending an additional +4 CP.
  • Suplex (1+ CP): You can perform the Knock Prone Chance Action for 1 CP instead of the usual 2. You can keep hold of the Grapple by spending an additional +1 CP.
  • Iron Grip (4 CP): While you are in control of a Grapple, you can spend your Instinct Action to impose a Major Disadvantage (-5) to all of your held target's Might or Reflexes-related Action Rolls until they break free, or the start of your next turn, whichever comes first.
  • Throw Combination (6+ CP): You slam your target on the ground, or into an object, multiple times. This attack deals your Grapple Strike Damage once for every 3 CP spent into it. If slammed into an object, add an amount of Damage equal to the AP value of the object's material.
  • Hurricane Throw (6 CP): Make a Grapple Attempt. You jump onto your target's shoulders and flip backwards, throwing them in the process. This attack deals twice your Reflexes in Bludgeoning Damage. If the target strikes an object, this Chance Action deals an additional amount of Damage equal to the AP value of the object's material.

docs.title: Role of the GM
docs.text:
As a GameMaster, your rulings are absolute, and you are the final arbiter of the game's world and rules. Despite the fact you'll often find yourself in control of the bad guys, your position need not be adversarial. Your goal in this system, as in all systems, is to facilitate your storytellers--aka--players in their adventures.

As the GM, you set the tone of the game, manage the overall narrative, balance encounters, and above all else, make an engaging and fun experience for everyone at the table; yourself included. Place problems in the world for your players, then sit back and let them figure out how to solve them. A great deal of the themes surrounding the Nomad series of novels is compelling moral dilemmas. Not every encounter need be a shootout on the docks or a car chase, fun as those are.

Have your players consider their Backgrounds and the stories about how their characters came to the point where your story begins, planning to incorporate those backstories into the plot where you can. Your players are always, 100%, unequivocally going to do things you didn't prepare for--and it's up to you to improvise in those moments to maintain a flexible narrative.

Remember to balance your pace, tone, and narrative to keep the game running smoothly. For some GMs, this means having an outline in which they can reliably set out a path for themselves in terms of major plot threads. For others, it means on-the-fly, seat-of-the-pants, figure it out as we go storytelling. Try not to overwhelm your players with too much action (which is just as dull as no action), too much roleplay (I'm not sure what the upper limit to this is, but I'm sure it exists), too much exploration, or too much item and money management.

Always remember that the game only exists when everyone at the table is engaged and having a good time. If at any point the rules get in the way of the fun, throw them out the window with some bath water, and adjust as needed.

There's a very special die that all GMs possess, system independent. It's called the d1. Also known as "I'm the GM. I said, and we agreed, that this is how this world works."

And, never forget the old adage: "No plan survives first contact with the Player Characters"

docs.title: Session-0
docs.text: A popular trend among TTRPG players recently is called a Session-0. This is generally a chance to build characters, consider concepts, and for the players and GM to come to a consensus on rules, abilities, themes, and included or banned subjects or topics. If you're starting with a new group or individuals who've never played together before, a Session-0 may not be a bad idea.

docs.title: Game Customization
docs.text: No game or system is one size fits all. If you have an idea for a mechanic or system that works better than what's in this book, feel free to homebrew some house rules. An important note to that, is to write down said rules, so that everyone can play on equal footing, and nobody can accuse you of making up the rules as you go (even though that's totally what we do — don't tell my players). Below are a few ideas for customizing your game.

docs.title: Game Difficulty Setting
docs.text:
First up is difficulty settings. Some GMs and players enjoy a solid power fantasy. Others prefer gritty realism. And, others find their home in absolutely brutal grimdark. This is a suggestion for how to accommodate each kind of playstyle, simply by changing the most important currency of the game, the Survivor Points, with which a character can modify and advance their characters. The lower difficulties allow for moderate to fast character growth, and an abundance of versatility, whereas the higher difficulties are unforgiving for mistakes in concept, build, or simply poor dice rolls.

Adjust as needed.

Easy Mode: Players gain 3 Survivor Points per level, starting at character creation. On taking points in Infection Level, players can choose the mutations their characters gain. Normal Mode: Players gain 2 Survivor Points per level, starting at character creation. Special Ability Mutations are randomly rolled by the GM. Hard Mode: Players gain 1 Survivor Point every odd level, starting at character creation. GM rolls 1d6 per character. Players can choose a number at or below the GM's roll to determine starting Infection Level. Nomad Mode: Players gain 1 Survivor Point every 4 levels, starting at character creation. GM rolls 1d6 per character, the result of which is each character's starting Infection Level. Virus Mode always imposes the Exhausted Condition after use.

docs.title: XP Growth or Milestones
docs.text:
A GM has, at their disposal, a flexible leveling system capable of both XP gain and Milestone leveling.

XP Rewards automatically scale with level, as each encounter can be divided into one of the five Threat Levels, and anything providing XP rewards would have a fail condition. For non-combat encounters you would like to provide XP for, reference the Advanced Action Roll chart, which is perfectly aligned to the Threat system.

Milestone Rewards are an equally equitable solution for player advancement, as it allows the GM and players to pace their progression via the story's beats, without having to rely on mathematics and increasingly lethal encounters.

A crafty GM may choose to use a hybrid of this, for added story flexibility when needed.

docs.title: House Rules
docs.text: No TTRPG system is universal without heavy lifting or a steep learning curve. And despite a few coming close with the vast, crunchy options at their disposal. This system was designed with simplicity and fast-paced, dynamic, and brutal combat in mind. If you'd like to implement rules that change the tone or flavor of the game, feel free and have fun.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
The base mechanics of this ruleset are designed for streamlined tactical play, with modularity in mind. While the core game centers around gritty survival scenarios and enhanced humans navigating a world they don't fit, its structure is flexible enough to support a variety of genres and tones with just a few focused tweaks.

  • Zombie Apocalypse: Infection Level becomes an almost purely negative item to track. Got bitten? Now there's a timer. Find a cure, or succumb. And the Transformation Stages instead represent Special Infected mutations, each one dragging the character further from humanity.
  • Martial Arts Mastery: Got an idea for a rogue warrior, fighting through underground circuits or in street duels? Let Infection Level fuel powerful finishing moves and ki bursts. Possibly even super-powered transformations. Use CP as a finishing move meter system. Chance Breaks become power surges & dangerous overloads.

The rules are a tool. Not a cage. If the game you want is in here somewhere, play it. It's not universal, but it can do a lot.

docs.title: Number of Dice
docs.text: This system’s mechanics were designed with ease of building and play in mind. Therefore, every roll in the game has been balanced around three six-sided dice (3d6). GMs and players can absolutely expand on this if they like, but as it is, those should be the absolute only dice needed in a Nomad campaign.

docs.title: Running Encounters
docs.text:
Combat Encounters, Building Monster Manual still, Horde Combat, Using the Environment, Special Challenges/Objectives

Encounter Goals 3d6Goal 1-2Breakthrough. The characters must pass through a dangerous area. 3-4Broker Peace. Two groups are fighting; the characters must step in and end the conflict, preferably witnout further violence. 5-6Persuade. Despite the enemy's aggression, the character's must convince the enemy to stop fighting them. 7-8Prevent Escape. The characters must kill or otherwise subdue the enemies before one or more can escape. 9-10Protection. The characters must protect a person or object that the other side hopes to destroy. 11-12Retrieval. The characters must take possession of an object or remove a person from the area of the encounter. 13-14Stealth. The characters must get through the encounter area without making the adversaries aware of their presence. This works especially well when the adversaries aren't necessarily bad, have hostages, or if the characters alert the enemies, even more will show up. 15-16Stop an Event. A dangerous ritual, assassination attempt, or other time sensitive event must be stopped before it's too late. 17-18Take Out One Target Only one of the creatures in the combat is the actual target; the others are either minions or innocents duped into serving them. Defeating the target is enough to end the combat.
  • Non-Combat Encounters: negotiations, puzzles, moral dilemmas.
  • Vehicle Encounters: Advice on running vehicle encounters.

[Additional Explanation/Expansion needed here.]

docs.title: Called Shots & Lingering Injuries
docs.text:
Sometimes you need surprise and violence. Sometimes you need the precision of a scalpel. The optional Called Shot system is for those moments when details matter.

This optional module is designed for GMs and players who want a more tactical, anatomical layer to combat. It is not required for standard play.

At its heart, a Called Shot lets a character aim at a specific part of an opponent's body to cause anch intended effect — like disabling a weapon hand, knocking out an opponent with a blow to the head, or delivering a lung-crushing punch.

The system is narrative-focused, as opposed to a simple increase in damage. It imposes Conditions, adds into Lingering Injuries, or could simply be used by the GM to add flavor to the flow of battles.

  • Attempting A Called Shot: The character must first spend a Minor Action to "Call" their shot. This is an opposed Action Roll:

Wits (Healing) vs Target's Reflexes (Acrobatics) or Willpower (Deception) (target's choice).

  • Attack Roll Penalties: Depending on where the character aims:
Minor Disadvantage (-2) For non-vital locations such as extremities or limbs. Major Disadvantage (-5) For vital points such as head, heart, or internal organs.
  • Resolution: If the Called Shot hits, the GM rolls or selects an appropriate result on the Hit Locations Table. For areas where the left or right side of the body are of importance during the random roll, the GM's Chance Die determines the side. An odd number indicates the left; whereas an even number indicates the right.

Hit Locations, Conditions, Healing

Outer body hit locations can go up to Tier 3 Conditions. All effects last until Healed via Surgery Action Roll. Unless specified in a special ability, only one Condition can be applied per hit.

All internal hit locations impose Tier 3 or above Conditions. All effects last until Healed via Surgery Action Roll. Unless specified in a special ability, only one Condition can be applied per hit.

docs.title: Non-Vital Locations
docs.text: Body Location 3d6 Hit Location 3-6 Head 7-8 Arm 9-12 Torso 13-14 Leg 15-18 Internal Head 3d6 Hit Location Condition(s) 3 Brain Confused (T5), Bleeding (T5), Cannot use Wits or Willpower 4-6 Eyes Impaired Sense (Sight), Stunned (Pain), Confusion (Blurred Vision) 7-8 Ears Impaired Sense (Hearing), Stunned (ringing disorientation), Poisoned (sickened) 9-12 Nose Bleeding, Confusion, Impaired Sense (smell) 13-14 Jaw Silenced, Fatigue (difficulty breathing, Tier 1) 15-18 Neck/Throat Silenced, Winded, Bleeding (Tier 3) Arms 3d6 Hit Location Condition 3-6 Elbow Weakness, Entangled (nerve damage), -2 to next Melee Attack Roll 7-8 Upper Arm Fatigue, Bleeding, Carry Capacity reduced by 50% 9-12 Forearm Entangled, Weakness, -2 to Defense against Melee Attacks. 13-14 Wrist Weakness, Slowed, Drop Held Item, Cannot be used for Action Rolls related to Might. 15-18 Fingers Slowed, Impaired Sense (touch), Entangled, -5 penalty to Grapple Action Rolls. Torso 3d6 Hit Location Condition 3 Upper Chest Vitals Winded (T5), Bleeding (T5) 4-6 Spine Stunned, Weakness, Paralyzed, Fatigue 7-8 Shoulder Entangled, Slowed, Weakness, Carry Capacity reduced by 50% 9-12 Ribcage Winded, Fatigued, Bleeding, Auto-fail next Suffocation Action Roll 13-14 Gut Poisoned, Confusion, Bleeding, Immobilized until the start of the Target's next Turn, Cannot take Major Actions 15-17 Crotch Stunned, Fear, Confusion, Speed reduced by 50% 18 Lower Chest Vitals Poisoned (T5), Bleeding (T4) Leg 3d6 Hit Location Condition 3-6 Knee Slowed, Entangled, Weakness, Auto-fail Action Rolls related to Jumping 7-8 Shin Slowed, Bleeding, Prone, Speed halved 9-12 Thigh Bleeding (Tier 3), Weakness, Jump Distance halved 13-14 Ankle Slowed, Entangled, Prone, Cannot Use Leg 15-18 Foot Slowed, Impaired Sense (balance), -5 on Action Rolls to resist being moved

docs.title: Infection and Mutation
docs.text: The Nomad Chronicle's defining feature—the Nano-Virus—represents both a gift and a curse. Players who choose to engage with this mechanic stand to gain incredible power, but at an accelerated risk. As the GM, you're responsible for managing this balance at the table narratively, mechanically, and thematically.

docs.title: Managing Infection Levels
docs.text:
Infection Level measures a character's integration with the Nano-Virus. Or, conversely, how well the Nano-Virus has adapted to enhancing that character's abilities. It is a double-edged sword—as it increases, the character gains more power, while the virus's influence grows harder to resist.

  • Use Infection as Progress and Threat: A high Infection Level shouldn't just be mechanical—it should change how the characters interact with the world around them. How NPCs perceive them, detect them, and react to them (e.g., automated defenses, infected enemies tracking them, viral sensors)

  • Optional Consequences: Permanent character loss may not be the best catch-all for all tables when it comes to transformation checks. If the consequences are too stringent, GMs are encouraged to work out ways to challenge their players while avoiding overuse of these high-powered abilities. Alternatively, if power fantasy is what you're after, feel free to adjust what consequences are best for your table.

docs.title: Virus Mode as a Narrative Tool
docs.text:
Virus Mode is a powerful ability—a last resort. A powerful, desperate transformation that can mean the difference between victory and death. Outside of the players activating it on their own terms, use it sparingly to maintain its impact.

  • Highlight Loss of Control Even when successful, the experience should feel dangerous and on edge. Describe the bloodlust, the way the character's body and mind prepare for violence. Describe how those around the character react in horror or awe.

  • Make it Feel Costly Every so often, remind players of the risk: loss of agency, side effects, exhaustion. If a character fails a transformation check, shift the tone—they are no longer a PC in that moment, they are a monster.

  • Create Moral Stakes Let Virus Mode moments complicate player choices. Saving your team by going berserk might earn their fear, not their respect. Especially if your shift caused losses on both sides.

docs.title: Environmental Considerations
docs.text: Games in this system rely and thrive on high tension, and one of the most effective tools to build that tension should be the world itself. The crafty GM will use the environment as a character all its own.

docs.title: Environmental Hazards
docs.text:
Environmental threats should force players to adapt—not necessarily by dealing more damage, but by introducing urgency.

  • Timed Hazards Toxic Air, Radiation, Extreme Temperatures, Chemical Weapons. These are things characters have to survive. Not just fight their way through.

  • Stacked Conditions Don't rely solely on combat to apply Conditions to your player's characters. Use the environment around them. If they're on an extended mission, you can stack on Fatigue. If they're exploring a secret laboratory in the arctic, they're likely to face Hypothermia. Nausea, sensory impairment. Get creative, and layer the pressure, so that encounters aren't the only time they feel the strain of their abilities.

docs.title: Item and Equipment Management
docs.text:
Let gear feel valuable and fragile.

  • Encourage Planning Players should consider what to bring into dangerous area. If they don't prepare for the cold or radiation, the environment should make them regret it.

  • Breakage is Drama Have batteries short out, guns jam due to lack of maintenance, or armor melting from an acid drip. Use the player's own equipment to create tense moments and force them to adapt.

docs.title: Resource Management
docs.text:
Survival in this game is a question of how long the characters can hold out, how creatively they can utilize their items and abilities, and how effectively they can synergize differences for teamwork.

  • Food, Ammo, Health Items: Got critical items to collect during an extended mission? Limit availability in key moments. Use scarcity to increase the drama — maybe one Health Stim is left, but there are two PCs near 0 Health.

  • Breathers = Strategy Since resting is used to recharge Health and life support, they are tactical decisions. Create times when doing one puts the other at risk.

  • Downtime Activities [under construction]

docs.title: Survivor Point Awards
docs.text:
[Revision Required]

The Survivor Point Checklist is meant to track a character's evolution not only in combat, but through exploration, roleplay, creativity, and personal growth. However, unchecked, it can quickly snowball into power creep. Here are a few guidelines to keep rewards balanced and meaningful:

Standard Award Rate: Each marked checkbox grants 1 SP, representing consistent character progression.

Bonus SP Awards: For moments of exceptional creativity, roleplaying, or dramatic risk-taking, consider awarding +1 additional SP. These moments should feel rare and earned.

Session Pacing: A party might earn between 2–5 SP per session (per character) depending on length and engagement. Use this to calibrate your pacing, accelerating or slowing as needed.

Milestone Option: For major narrative breakthroughs or completed arcs, consider awarding a flat +2 to +6 SP to all players, separate from checkboxes.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
docs.text: Even the best GMs hit snags. Here’s how to navigate the most common issues:

docs.title: Railroading
docs.text:
• The Problem: You’ve crafted a story. The players ignore it.
  • The Fix: Instead of writing a plot, write consequences. Let player actions shape the world. Outline motivations for key NPCs and factions so they can respond naturally, not just by following your script.
  • Tip: Ask yourself: “What’s this scene about?” If you know the stakes, you can let the outcome vary.

docs.title: Power Imbalance
docs.text:
• The Problem: One character overshadows the others mechanically or narratively.
  • The Fix: Don’t nerf. Instead, spotlight others through environmental challenges or social scenes where different Abilities shine. Give the high-powered character a complication every so often that makes their strength a potential liability (e.g., public fear, instability, or mutation risk). But, don't overdo this.

docs.title: Analysis Paralysis
docs.text:
• The Problem: You, or the Players freeze, debating too many options or unable to make a decision.
  • The Fix: Add time pressure. Introduce a ticking clock or collapsing environment to force choices. Give incomplete intel — enough to act, but not to be certain.
  • Tip: Limit options to three visible paths. Let players discover more only if they go looking.
  • Tip 2: Use a tool, such as an oracle system (i.e.: themed random roll charts, as made popular by Mythic: Game-Master Emulator), asking questions and interpreting the results to find your next story beat.

docs.title: GM Burnout
docs.text:
• The Problem: You’re tired. You’re dreading game night.
  • The Fix: Simplify. Run a one-shot with pregens. Let players drive the narrative for a session. Skip to the next arc if things have stalled. You don’t owe anyone perfection — just effort.
  • Tip: Check ahead of time if any of your players might like to GM a one-shot of some kind. Let them take the reins for a session or two. Or, if necessary, simply take a break.

docs.title: The Nomad Chronicles
docs.text: A compact, narrative-focused tabletop roleplaying game about exploration, conflict, and small groups forced to make hard choices in a changing world.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: TNC World is a rules-light system that centers on character motives, player-driven scenes, and fast resolution. Emphasis is on story beats and consequences rather than simulation: tense choices, risky plans, and the social costs of survival.

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
• 1 Gamemaster (GM) and 2–6 players
  • 1–2 six-sided dice (d6) per player (or a dice pool app)
  • Character sheets (one per player)
  • Tokens or markers for resource tracking

docs.title: Core themes
docs.text:
• Survival vs. Ethics: tradeoffs between doing what's needed and who you become doing it
  • Small-group dynamics: leadership, trust, and betrayal under pressure
  • Mystery of the world: fragments to discover, consequences to unwrap

docs.title: How to play (quick)
docs.text:
1. Create characters: pick a role, a driving desire, and one secret.
  2. The GM frames a scene and prompts player actions.
  3. Players declare an action and roll d6 (simple success/failure or use a small pool).
  4. GM describes results and advances threats or rewards.
  5. Track resources and complications; use short downtime between scenes to develop characters.

docs.title: Safety and session zero
docs.text:
• Run a short session zero to set tone, boundaries, and consent.
  • Use safety tools (X-card, lines & veils) and establish hard limits.
  • Agree on the level of violence, horror, and mature themes beforehand.

docs.title: For the GM
docs.text:
• Start small: one clear objective and one strong antagonist.
  • Keep scenes short and move the spotlight between players.
  • Use complications to force meaningful choices rather than arbitrary penalties.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Read the rules reference for character generation, mechanics, and combat.
  • Try a one-shot scenario to learn pacing and flow.
  • Share feedback and house rules with your table.

docs.title: The Nomad Initiative
docs.text:
Service in honor. Hope in Brotherhood. Victory in Sacrifice. This is what it means to be a Nomad. The Nomad Initiative began as an experimental military unit comprised of otherwise ordinary people intentionally infected with what is known as the Nano-Virus.

The Nano-Virus enhances the human body beyond its normal limits. It provides some with additional strength, resilience, or speed. As such, it turned its initial units into powerful weapons of war. But, as with all experiments, there were side effects. Some that were infected failed to assimilate the virus correctly, becoming grotesque and dangerous creatures.

The program was eventually scrapped by the military, but its creator continued in secret, perfecting his creation. Survivors of that initial program have become parents. And, unknown to those in command, many of their progeny carry a successful iteration of the virus within their blood.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
The Nomad Chronicles system assumes a modern level of technology, with a little dabble into future tech. Power armor exists but is extremely experimental. Only a few suits exist, and they're jealously guarded. Handheld frictionless railgun weapons as well.

For the most part, however, whatever you can find in the modern world, you should have access to in this system. The core of the Nomad Chronicles is an exploration of survival, resilience, friendship, and struggling with your inner demons. Players and Game Masters (GMs) are encouraged to explore these themes in their games. Though, as with all systems; rules, themes, and other such situations, are guidelines secondary to your enjoyment of the game.

Given the history behind the Nomad Initiative and the Nano-Virus, quite a few kinds of stories are possible using this system. Perhaps the players are recruits in a black-ops unit tasked with overthrowing a nation. Perhaps they're college friends starting their own mercenary outfit. Or, as in the initial Nomad novel, they could even be attempting to survive in the event of a large-scale failed infection.

In all cases, it's likely the players will need to emphasize teamwork, strategy, and creative problem-solving. At times, it will take that and more to succeed in the mission.

docs.title: The Structure
docs.text:
The mechanics for this system are loosely based on the Adventure Game Engine (AGE) 3d6 ruleset. Let's take a moment or two to address how this system differs, is structured, and how to use it. Because, as we all know, Tabletop Role-Playing Games (TTRPGs) are more about the application of knowledge, rather than simply having a lot of knowledge.

System Foundations: The Nomad Chronicles (TNC) draws inspiration from the 3d6-based resolution mechanics popularized by Green Ronin Publishing's Adventure Game Engine (AGE). TNC diverges substantially, however, in mechanics, execution, and structure.

  • Ability Scores: TNC uses six core abilities with a unique range and scaling, as opposed to AGE's nine core abilities.
  • Action Resolution: While both systems use 3d6, TNC introduces the Chance Die, that feeds from and interacts with a persistent Chance Pool for tactical ability use rather than triggering immediate-use Chance Actions.
  • Progression: TNC features Experience Points and Survivor Points for advancement, allowing granular character growth through ability improvements, Traits, gear proficiencies, infection level, and mutations.
  • Traits & Expertise: TNC separates Traits (small bonuses) from deep, multi-tiered Expertise, creating a hybrid between class features and skill trees.
  • Combat: :: TNC emphasizes cinematic, condition-based combat with custom mechanics for cover, knockback, mutation states, and tactical positioning.
  • Thematic Setting: While the concept of super soldiers is nothing new to fiction, the viral nature of the Nomads, alongside item degradation, Requisition Points (RP), and narrative "Virus Mode" moments, provides a wholly distinct flavor and gameplay experience.

docs.title: Vehicles
docs.text: Sometimes, a fight spills farther than you may have anticipated. Sometimes, a character needs to get in or out of a situation in a hurry. This subchapter includes vehicle rules, statistics, and modifications for resolving actions where vehicles are involved. That said, vehicles can be damaged. See Equipment: Damage & Destruction, pg. 85 subchapter for more information.

docs.title: Maneuvering
docs.text:
Every vehicle has a Maneuver Modifier, indicating how much some vehicles are easier to maneuver than others. Additionally, every vehicle has a Top Speed, indicated on the chart below. There are times when two vehicles attempt to outmaneuver one another, but one happens to be significantly faster or more nimble.

  • Penalty for Speed: Each speed category above Street Speed imposes a cumulative -1 penalty to Maneuver checks. Each category below Street Speed grants a cumulative +1 bonus.
  • Action Types:
  • Minor Action: Simple maneuvers (e.g., driving straight, gentle turns).
  • Major Action: Complex maneuvers (e.g., sharp turns, drifting, evasive actions).

Maneuver Action Rolls: Reflexes + relevant skill (Riding, Driving, Piloting) + Maneuver Score +/− Speed Penalty

docs.title: Speed Categories
docs.text:
Speed Category Speed Range (mph) Speed (yards/round) Collision Damage Stationary 0 0 0 Alley Speed 1-25 mph 12 2 Street Speed 26-50 mph 25 3 Highway Speed 51-70 mph 32 4 High Speed 71+ mph 36+ 5 Reverse Speed One category below Top Speed. - 1

Drivers can use a minor action to change speed categories, shifting up or down by 1 category at a time. Only certain modifications can increase a vehicle's Speed Category beyond High Speed.

Any Air Vehicle that cannot effectively hover, must maintain Highway or High Speed. At Street Speed, the aircraft begins descending. At Alley Speed or below, the aircraft stalls, dropping into a freefall that requires a CR18 Piloting (Reflexes) test to recover from without crashing the vehicle.

docs.title: Vehicle Stats
